-Працю́ю вдома.
-I work at home.
-
-Слу́хаю ра́діо.
-I listen to the radio.
-
-Пишу́ лист.
-I write a letter.
-
 > [!warning]
-> Do not use the dictionary form, the one ending in **-ти**, when talking about what you are doing right now. Always drop the **-ти** and add the correct personal ending.
+> The highly useful verb **пита́ти** (to ask) is a notable exception in how it connects to other words in a sentence. Do not use it to say "ask someone" just yet, because pointing the action at a person requires a different and more complex case rule. For now, only use it safely with the phrase **про це** (about this).
+> **Я пита́ю про це (I ask about this).**
+> **Він пита́є про це (He asks about this).**
 
-Тепер ми поєднуємо ці слова з простими предметами. Це неживі предмети.
-Now we combine these words with simple objects. These are inanimate objects.
+Let us build simple, complete sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change at all when they become the direct target of an action.
 
-Чита́ю журнал.
-I read a magazine.
+**Я чита́ю журна́л (I read a magazine).**
+**Він пи́ше лист (He writes a letter).**
+**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо (We listen to the radio).**
+**Вони́ гра́ють у го́льф (They play golf).**
 
-Пишу́ повідомлення.
-I write a message.
+You can also use these exact same verbs to clearly describe your daily routine and work activities.
 
-Він зна́є пра́вило.
-He knows the rule.
+**Я працю́ю над проєктом (I work on a project).**
+**По́тім я відпочива́ю вдо́ма (Then I rest at home).**
+**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус (We wait for the bus).**
+**Він чека́є на ві́дповідь (He waits for an answer).**
+**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву (You study the language).**
+**Я вивча́ю слова́ (I study words).**
+**Я не розумі́ю (I do not understand).**
+**Вони́ ду́мають бага́то (They think a lot).**
 
-Ось кілька міні-діалогів для практики. Важливий момент — це закінчення.
-Here are a few mini-dialogues for practice. An important point is the endings.
+By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information about your day.
 
-— Ти слу́хаєш ра́діо? (Do you listen to the radio?)
-— Ні, я слу́хаю подкаст. (No, I listen to a podcast.)
-
-— Що він ро́бить? (What is he doing?)
-— Він чита́є журнал. (He is reading a magazine.)
-
-— Ви працю́єте вдома? (Do you work at home?)
-— Так, ми працю́ємо вдома. (Yes, we work at home.)
-
-Ми можемо описати наш день. Ці речення дуже корисні для щоденного життя.
-We can describe our day. These sentences are very useful for daily life.
-
-Ми відпочива́ємо. (We rest.)
-Гра́ємо в і́гри. (We play games.)
-Вивча́ємо слова́. (We study words.)
-Студе́нт чита́є текст. (The student reads the text.)
-Брат чека́є на автобус. (The brother waits for the bus.)
-Я розумі́ю текст. (I understand the text.)
-Ми ду́маємо багато. (We think a lot.)
-Вони́ пита́ють про проєкт. (They ask about the project.)
-
-Practice reading these short phrases aloud. Hearing the subject together with the correct verb ending (like **ми** + **-ємо**) will help you internalize the pattern.
-
 ## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)
 
-You now hold what we can call the "Master Key" to the Ukrainian language. This single conjugation pattern unlocks hundreds of everyday verbs. Once you master the basic endings for the pronouns "я," "ти," "він," "вона," "ми," "ви," and "вони," you will suddenly find yourself able to express a vast array of thoughts, daily routines, and specific actions without needing to learn entirely new grammatical structures. 
+You have learned an incredibly powerful grammatical tool today. The First Conjugation pattern is essentially like a universal master key to the language. Once you thoroughly learn and internalize the **-ю, -єш, -є, -ємо, -єте, -ють** endings, you can instantly unlock and use hundreds of everyday Ukrainian verbs. You simply need to learn the basic infinitive form from the dictionary, and you will immediately know exactly how to use it in a real, functioning sentence.
 
-В українській культурі мова дуже важлива.
-In Ukrainian culture, language is very important.
+You learned how to clearly say what you do: **я чита́ю** (I read), **я працю́ю** (I work), and **я зна́ю** (I know). You also learned the vitally important stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending effectively contains all the necessary subject information within itself. 
 
-Є дуже відоме прислів'я про це.
-There is a very famous proverb about this.
+> [!culture]
+> There is a famous, profoundly beautiful Ukrainian proverb: «Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю, а люди́ну по мо́ві» (A bird is known by its feathers, a person by their speech). It broadly means that the way you speak reveals a great deal about your true character, your overall education, and your deep respect for others. Taking the dedicated time to learn and apply the correct grammatical verb endings shows deep respect for the language and genuinely helps you speak much more beautifully and authentically. 
 
-Нова мова — нова реальність.
-A new language is a new reality.
+You are no longer just pointing at and naming static objects in a room. You are actively describing ongoing actions, personal habits, and daily routines. This represents a truly massive step forward in your Ukrainian language learning journey. The transition from English instructional scaffolding to real, active Ukrainian language immersion begins with these simple, foundational action statements.
 
-Говорити красиво — це важливо. Коли ви використовуєте правильні закінчення, ви звучите дуже природно.
-Speaking beautifully is important. When you use the correct endings, you sound very natural.
+Here are a few essential self-check questions to directly help you review and solidify the crucial material from this lesson.
 
-This proverb reminds us that the way we speak leaves a lasting impression. While native speakers will understand you if you simply say "Я читати" (I to read), taking the time to use the correctly conjugated ending ("Я читаю") shows that you are actively engaging with the melody and logic of Ukrainian. Good grammar isn't just about following strict rules; it's about connecting with the language naturally and showing appreciation for the local culture. 
-
-Every time you conjugate a verb correctly, you are building a bridge between yourself and native Ukrainian speakers. You are telling them that you care enough to speak their language the way it is genuinely meant to be spoken. Furthermore, because Ukrainian is a pro-drop language, your correct verb endings allow you to speak more efficiently. Dropping the personal pronoun sounds much more natural and confident. It proves that you are moving beyond simple memorization and starting to truly feel the rhythm of the language. This natural fluency is the ultimate goal of learning any language.
-
----
-
-## Підсумок
-
-Тепер ви знаєте, як працює перша група. Ви знаєте, як утворювати речення тільки з дією.
-Now you know how First Conjugation verbs work in the present tense. You know how to drop pronouns for natural speech and how to form basic action sentences.
-
-1. Яке закінчення для «ми»? (What is the "ми" ending?) — The ending is -ємо.
-2. Чи завжди потрібно використовувати «я»? (Do you always need to use "я"?) — No, the verb ending often makes the pronoun unnecessary.
-3. Яка основа слова «читати»? (What is the stem of "читати"?) — Drop the -ти to get "чита-".
-4. Як сказати «I am reading»? (How do you say "I am reading"?) — Читаю.
-
----
+1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun **ми** (we) in the First Conjugation? — The correct and completely regular ending is **-ємо**, for example: **ми чита́ємо** (we read).
+2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun **я** (I) in a sentence to be understood? — No, the verb ending **-ю** clearly indicates the subject on its own, so you can often smoothly drop the pronoun entirely.
+3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb **чита́ти** (to read)? — You drop the **-ти** ending from the standard dictionary infinitive to safely get the stem **чита-**.
+4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? — You can say **Я чита́ю** or, more naturally, simply **Чита́ю**.

<!-- SCOPE
Covers: First Conjugation (-ати/-яти) verbs in the present tense, imperfective aspect introduction, and pro-drop sentences.
Not covered:
  - Second Conjugation verbs → a1-16
  - Questions and Negation → a1-18
-->

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Ми вже знаємо, як називати речі.
> We already know how to name things.
>
> Тепер ми діємо.
> Now we act.
>
> Learning to use verbs correctly transforms your Ukrainian from a list of objects into a living, breathing language. Actions give your words power and momentum.

## Вступ (Introduction)

Ми вже знаємо, як описувати речі. Це добрий старт. Але мова — це також дія. Ми діємо щодня.
We already know how to describe things. This is a good start. But language is also action. We act every day.

Це стіл. Це моя книга.
This is a table. This is my book.

Ми знаємо багато слів.
We know many words.

Тепер ми читаємо текст. Ми працюємо вдома.
Now we read the text. We work at home.

In English, a verb mostly stays the same regardless of who is acting (I read, you read, we read). In Ukrainian, **verbs change their endings** to match the person performing the action. This might seem challenging at first, but it is actually a beautiful system that makes sentences shorter and more efficient. Once you understand the core pattern, you will not have to memorize every single form individually. The logic will guide you. 

Ми хочемо вільно говорити. Тому ми вивчаємо дієслова (verbs). Вони описують наші щоденні дії.
We want to speak fluently. Therefore we study verbs. They describe our daily actions.

Сьогодні ми вивчаємо слово «читати» (to read). Ми використовуємо його правильно.
Today we study the word "читати" (to read). We use it correctly.

> [!culture]
> The rich literary tradition in Ukraine means verbs like **чита́ти** (to read) and **писа́ти** (to write) hold a special place in the language. Knowing how to use them correctly connects you directly to centuries of history and thousands of amazing stories, poems, and songs. Reading is highly valued.

## Презентація (Presentation)

У цій групі багато важливих слів. Це перша велика група. Слова зазвичай мають закінчення «-ати» у словнику.
There are many important words in this group. This is the first big group. The words usually have the "-ати" ending in the dictionary.

Ось кілька прикладів:
Here are a few examples:

* **чита́ти** — to read
* **зна́ти** — to know
* **працюва́ти** — to work
* **слу́хати** — to listen
* **ду́мати** — to think
* **чека́ти** — to wait
* **гра́ти** — to play

Ми повинні змінити закінчення. Закінчення показує, хто виконує дію.
We must change the ending. The ending shows who is performing the action.

Правило дуже просте. Спочатку ми забираємо закінчення «-ти». Залишається основа. Потім ми додаємо нове закінчення.
The rule is very simple. First we take away the "-ти" ending. The stem remains. Then we add a new ending.

Наприклад, основа слова «читати» — це «чита-».
For example, the stem of the word "to read" is "чита-".

Look at the complete conjugation pattern for the verb **чита́ти**:

| Pronoun | Ending | Verb Form | English |
|---------|--------|-----------|---------|
| **я** | -ю | я чита́ю | I read / I am reading |
| **ти** | -єш | ти чита́єш | you read (singular, informal) |
| **він/вона/воно** | -є | він чита́є | he reads |
| **ми** | -ємо | ми чита́ємо | we read |
| **ви** | -єте | ви чита́єте | you read (plural or formal) |
| **вони** | -ють | вони чита́ють | they read |

Український теперішній час має два значення. Це регулярна дія. І це дія зараз.
The Ukrainian present tense has two meanings. It is a regular action. And it is an action now.

Я чита́ю журнал.
I read a magazine. / I am reading a magazine.

Слово «працювати» має таке саме правило. Ми забираємо «-ти».
For another word, "працювати" (to work), the rule is the same. We remove "-ти".

Я працю́ю.
I work.

Вона́ працю́є вдома.
She works at home. / She is working at home.

Ми працю́ємо багато.
We work a lot.

Вони́ працю́ють.
They work.

> [!tip]
> Українські слова-дії мають багато інформації. Закінчення ідеально відповідає особі. Тому додаткове слово (я, ти) часто не потрібне.
> Ukrainian action words have a lot of information. The ending perfectly matches the person. Therefore, the extra word (I, you) is often not needed.

Ви можете сказати «Я знаю», але українці часто кажуть просто «Знаю». Закінчення «-ю» — це сигнал для «я».
You can say "I know", but Ukrainians often say simply "I know". The "-ю" ending is a signal for "I".

Зна́ю це.
I know this.

Розумі́ємо все.
We understand everything.

Once you learn these endings (**-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, **-ють**), you can conjugate hundreds of common Ukrainian verbs that end in **-ати**.

## Практика (Practice)

Now we will build more sentences and correct some common habits.

Англомовні люди часто роблять дві типові помилки. Вони використовують словниковий варіант. Це звучить дуже неприродно.
English-speaking people often make two typical mistakes. They use the dictionary version. This sounds very unnatural.

The first common error is what we call "Infinitive Abuse". Beginners might say "Я читати" (I to read). This sounds very confusing in Ukrainian. You must always change the ending.

Я чита́ю текст.
I read the text.

Ти слу́хаєш ра́діо.
You listen to the radio.

Він гра́є на гітарі.
He plays the guitar.

Друга помилка — це зайві слова для особи (я, ти). Повторювати «я» чи «ми» в кожному реченні не потрібно.
The second mistake is extra words for the person (I, you). Repeating "I" or "we" in every sentence is not necessary.

In English, you must always say the pronoun. You cannot just say "Work at home." You must say "I work at home." In Ukrainian, repeating "я" or "ми" every single time sounds robotic and stiff.

Працю́ю вдома.
I work at home.

Слу́хаю ра́діо.
I listen to the radio.

Пишу́ лист.
I write a letter.

> [!warning]
> Do not use the dictionary form, the one ending in **-ти**, when talking about what you are doing right now. Always drop the **-ти** and add the correct personal ending.

Тепер ми поєднуємо ці слова з простими предметами. Це неживі предмети.
Now we combine these words with simple objects. These are inanimate objects.

Чита́ю журнал.
I read a magazine.

Пишу́ повідомлення.
I write a message.

Він зна́є пра́вило.
He knows the rule.

Ось кілька міні-діалогів для практики. Важливий момент — це закінчення.
Here are a few mini-dialogues for practice. An important point is the endings.

— Ти слу́хаєш ра́діо? (Do you listen to the radio?)
— Ні, я слу́хаю подкаст. (No, I listen to a podcast.)

— Що він ро́бить? (What is he doing?)
— Він чита́є журнал. (He is reading a magazine.)

— Ви працю́єте вдома? (Do you work at home?)
— Так, ми працю́ємо вдома. (Yes, we work at home.)

Ми можемо описати наш день. Ці речення дуже корисні для щоденного життя.
We can describe our day. These sentences are very useful for daily life.

Ми відпочива́ємо. (We rest.)
Гра́ємо в і́гри. (We play games.)
Вивча́ємо слова́. (We study words.)
Студе́нт чита́є текст. (The student reads the text.)
Брат чека́є на автобус. (The brother waits for the bus.)
Я розумі́ю текст. (I understand the text.)
Ми ду́маємо багато. (We think a lot.)
Вони́ пита́ють про проєкт. (They ask about the project.)

Practice reading these short phrases aloud. Hearing the subject together with the correct verb ending (like **ми** + **-ємо**) will help you internalize the pattern.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

You now hold what we can call the "Master Key" to the Ukrainian language. This single conjugation pattern unlocks hundreds of everyday verbs. Once you master the basic endings for the pronouns "я," "ти," "він," "вона," "ми," "ви," and "вони," you will suddenly find yourself able to express a vast array of thoughts, daily routines, and specific actions without needing to learn entirely new grammatical structures. 

В українській культурі мова дуже важлива.
In Ukrainian culture, language is very important.

Є дуже відоме прислів'я про це.
There is a very famous proverb about this.

Нова мова — нова реальність.
A new language is a new reality.

Говорити красиво — це важливо. Коли ви використовуєте правильні закінчення, ви звучите дуже природно.
Speaking beautifully is important. When you use the correct endings, you sound very natural.

This proverb reminds us that the way we speak leaves a lasting impression. While native speakers will understand you if you simply say "Я читати" (I to read), taking the time to use the correctly conjugated ending ("Я читаю") shows that you are actively engaging with the melody and logic of Ukrainian. Good grammar isn't just about following strict rules; it's about connecting with the language naturally and showing appreciation for the local culture. 

Every time you conjugate a verb correctly, you are building a bridge between yourself and native Ukrainian speakers. You are telling them that you care enough to speak their language the way it is genuinely meant to be spoken. Furthermore, because Ukrainian is a pro-drop language, your correct verb endings allow you to speak more efficiently. Dropping the personal pronoun sounds much more natural and confident. It proves that you are moving beyond simple memorization and starting to truly feel the rhythm of the language. This natural fluency is the ultimate goal of learning any language.

---

## Підсумок

Тепер ви знаєте, як працює перша група. Ви знаєте, як утворювати речення тільки з дією.
Now you know how First Conjugation verbs work in the present tense. You know how to drop pronouns for natural speech and how to form basic action sentences.

1. Яке закінчення для «ми»? (What is the "ми" ending?) — The ending is -ємо.
2. Чи завжди потрібно використовувати «я»? (Do you always need to use "я"?) — No, the verb ending often makes the pronoun unnecessary.
3. Яка основа слова «читати»? (What is the stem of "читати"?) — Drop the -ти to get "чита-".
4. Як сказати «I am reading»? (How do you say "I am reading"?) — Читаю.

---

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Learning how to conjugate verbs unlocks your ability to describe actions and routines. Instead of just naming static objects around you, you can now confidently say what you do with them every single day. This is the foundation of conversational fluency.

## Вступ (Introduction)

Until now, we have mostly looked at the static world around us. We learned how to identify objects, describe their colors, and point out exactly where they are located. Ми ба́чили ре́чі (We saw objects). Ми зна́ємо слова́ (We know words). We learned how to say what things are. Now, it is time to bring those everyday objects to life with dynamic action.

In this important lesson, you will learn how to describe your daily actions. You will transition from simply saying "this is a book" to proudly stating "I read a book." This means we are finally diving into verbs. A verb is an action word. In the Ukrainian language, verbs are highly dynamic. They change their endings depending on who exactly is doing the action. This fundamental process of changing verb endings is called conjugation. It might seem completely new at first, but it follows very logical and predictable patterns.

You will start with the most common and regular group of verbs. This vast group is often called the First Conjugation. Most of these verbs end in **-ати** in their dictionary form. When you look up an action in a dictionary, you will always find this form. It is called the infinitive. For example, the verb **чита́ти** (to read) is an infinitive. It represents the pure idea of the action before anyone actually performs it. 

You already know many things about this language. **Тепе́р ви зна́єте бі́льше (Now you know more).** The verb **зна́ти** (to know) also belongs to this very same conjugation group. Another incredibly useful and high-frequency verb is **працюва́ти** (to work). These verbs describe ongoing actions or regular habits. They tell us what you are doing right now, or what you usually do every single day. This concept is known as the imperfective aspect.

> [!culture]
> The verb **чита́ти** (to read) has incredibly deep roots in Ukrainian history. In the year 1574, a famous printer named Ivan Fedorov published a book called the *Apostol* in the beautiful city of Lviv. It was the very first printed book in Ukraine. This historic event marked the beginning of a rich, centuries-old literacy tradition. Knowing how to read and write has always been highly valued and deeply respected in traditional Ukrainian culture.

Let us explore exactly how these verbs work in practice. You will soon see that the grammatical pattern is logical, remarkably consistent, and easy to follow.

## Презентація (Presentation)

To use a verb in a real, spoken sentence, you must change its ending. This change tells the listener exactly who is performing the action without needing extra context or explanation. Let us look closely at the core pattern for First Conjugation verbs.

Think of the infinitive form as having two separate structural parts: the stem and the ending. For regular verbs like **чита́ти** (to read), the infinitive ending is **-ти**. The stem is everything that comes right before that ending. For this specific word, the stem is **чита-**. 

To conjugate the verb correctly, you drop the **-ти** and add a personal ending. These personal endings always match the pronouns: **я** (I), **ти** (you singular), **він/вона/воно** (he/she/it), **ми** (we), **ви** (you plural/formal), and **вони** (they).

Here is the complete present tense pattern using the verb **чита́ти** (to read):

| Pronoun | Stem | Ending | Full Verb | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **я** | чита- | **-ю** | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| **ти** | чита- | **-єш** | **ти чита́єш** | you read (singular) |
| **він/вона** | чита- | **-є** | **він/вона чита́є** | he/she reads |
| **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural/formal) |
| **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |

Notice carefully that the endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This specific grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can apply it to hundreds of different verbs in the Ukrainian language without worrying about exceptions. 

Let us apply the exact same pattern to the verb **зна́ти** (to know). Drop the **-ти** ending to isolate the stem **зна-**.

**Я зна́ю все (I know everything).**
**Він зна́є пра́вду (He knows the truth).**
**Ми не зна́ємо (We do not know).**
**Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило (They know the rule).**

> [!tip]
> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. You cannot just say "Read a book" if you mean "I read a book." In Ukrainian, however, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** always and exclusively means "I". Because of this built-in grammatical information, you can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and very common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation. 

The important verb **працюва́ти** (to work) also belongs to this group, but it has a slight spelling change. For verbs ending in **-ювати**, the **-юва-** part shortens to **-ю-** before you add the ending. 

**Я працю́ю вдо́ма (I work at home).**
**Вони́ працю́ють бага́то (They work a lot).**
**Ми працю́ємо ра́зом (We work together).**

These verbs describe continuous, ongoing processes. If you say **я працю́ю** (I work / I am working), it means you are engaged in the activity right now, or it is your regular daily habit. Ukrainian does not have a separate continuous tense like "am working" in English. The simple present tense smoothly covers both meanings.

## Практика (Practice)

Now it is time to practice building your own sentences. Many English speakers make a very common structural mistake when they first start using Ukrainian verbs. They try to use the dictionary form, the infinitive, directly in their sentences. They might mistakenly say **Я чита́ти** when they actually mean "I read." This is grammatically incorrect and sounds confusing to native speakers. You must always change the ending to match the person performing the action.

Remember to constantly replace the incorrect infinitive with the properly conjugated form. 
Instead of saying **Я писа́ти**, you must say **Я пишу́** (I write).
Instead of saying **Я слу́хати**, you must say **Я слу́хаю** (I listen).
Instead of saying **Я гра́ти**, you must say **Я гра́ю** (I play).
Instead of saying **Я ду́мати**, you must say **Я ду́маю** (I think).

Let us practice with a few more common verbs. Read these sentences carefully and observe the endings.

**Я слу́хаю му́зику (I listen to music).**
**Вона́ слу́хає ра́діо (She listens to the radio).**
**Ти ува́жно слу́хаєш (You listen carefully).**
**Ми слу́хаємо нови́ни (We listen to the news).**

Another very common habit for English speakers is overusing pronouns. Because strict English grammar requires "I", "you", or "we" in nearly every single sentence, you might be tempted to say **Я чита́ю. Я працю́ю. Я зна́ю.** While this is technically correct and completely understandable, it sounds highly repetitive and slightly unnatural in casual Ukrainian conversation.

Try reading these short descriptive sequences without the constant repetition of "я". 

**Чита́ю нови́ни. Працю́ю. Відпочива́ю. (I read the news. I work. I rest.)**
**Слу́хаю ра́діо. Ду́маю. (I listen to the radio. I think.)**
**Пишу́ лист. Чека́ю. (I write a letter. I wait.)**

Notice how much smoother and more native it sounds. The verb endings do all the heavy lifting for you, making your speech sound flowing and confident.

> [!warning]
> The highly useful verb **пита́ти** (to ask) is a notable exception in how it connects to other words in a sentence. Do not use it to say "ask someone" just yet, because pointing the action at a person requires a different and more complex case rule. For now, only use it safely with the phrase **про це** (about this).
> **Я пита́ю про це (I ask about this).**
> **Він пита́є про це (He asks about this).**

Let us build simple, complete sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change at all when they become the direct target of an action.

**Я чита́ю журна́л (I read a magazine).**
**Він пи́ше лист (He writes a letter).**
**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо (We listen to the radio).**
**Вони́ гра́ють у го́льф (They play golf).**

You can also use these exact same verbs to clearly describe your daily routine and work activities.

**Я працю́ю над проєктом (I work on a project).**
**По́тім я відпочива́ю вдо́ма (Then I rest at home).**
**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус (We wait for the bus).**
**Він чека́є на ві́дповідь (He waits for an answer).**
**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву (You study the language).**
**Я вивча́ю слова́ (I study words).**
**Я не розумі́ю (I do not understand).**
**Вони́ ду́мають бага́то (They think a lot).**

By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information about your day.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

You have learned an incredibly powerful grammatical tool today. The First Conjugation pattern is essentially like a universal master key to the language. Once you thoroughly learn and internalize the **-ю, -єш, -є, -ємо, -єте, -ють** endings, you can instantly unlock and use hundreds of everyday Ukrainian verbs. You simply need to learn the basic infinitive form from the dictionary, and you will immediately know exactly how to use it in a real, functioning sentence.

You learned how to clearly say what you do: **я чита́ю** (I read), **я працю́ю** (I work), and **я зна́ю** (I know). You also learned the vitally important stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending effectively contains all the necessary subject information within itself. 

> [!culture]
> There is a famous, profoundly beautiful Ukrainian proverb: «Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю, а люди́ну по мо́ві» (A bird is known by its feathers, a person by their speech). It broadly means that the way you speak reveals a great deal about your true character, your overall education, and your deep respect for others. Taking the dedicated time to learn and apply the correct grammatical verb endings shows deep respect for the language and genuinely helps you speak much more beautifully and authentically. 

You are no longer just pointing at and naming static objects in a room. You are actively describing ongoing actions, personal habits, and daily routines. This represents a truly massive step forward in your Ukrainian language learning journey. The transition from English instructional scaffolding to real, active Ukrainian language immersion begins with these simple, foundational action statements.

Here are a few essential self-check questions to directly help you review and solidify the crucial material from this lesson.

1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun **ми** (we) in the First Conjugation? — The correct and completely regular ending is **-ємо**, for example: **ми чита́ємо** (we read).
2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun **я** (I) in a sentence to be understood? — No, the verb ending **-ю** clearly indicates the subject on its own, so you can often smoothly drop the pronoun entirely.
3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb **чита́ти** (to read)? — You drop the **-ти** ending from the standard dictionary infinitive to safely get the stem **чита-**.
4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? — You can say **Я чита́ю** or, more naturally, simply **Чита́ю**.

This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.

args.content:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: First Conjugation (-ати/-яти) in present tense, basic SVO sentences with inanimate objects.
Not covered:
  - Second Conjugation verbs → a1-16
  - Questions and negation → a1-18
-->

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Learning how to conjugate verbs unlocks your ability to describe actions and routines. Instead of simply naming static objects around you, you can now confidently say what you do with them every single day. This is the foundation of conversational fluency.

## Вступ (Introduction)

**Ми вивча́ємо слова́. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Тепе́р ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
We study words. We study the language. Now we know a lot.

**Я чита́ю кни́гу. Ти чита́єш нови́ни.**
I read a book. You read the news.

In this important lesson, you will learn how to describe your daily actions and habits. You will transition from simply naming objects to proudly stating what you actively do with them. A verb is an action word. In the Ukrainian language, verbs change their endings depending on who exactly is doing the action. This fundamental process of changing verb endings is called conjugation. It follows very logical and predictable patterns.

You will start with the most common and regular group of verbs. This large group is called the First Conjugation. Most of these verbs end in **-ати** in their dictionary form. This base form is called the infinitive. For example, the verb **чита́ти** is an infinitive. 

The verb **зна́ти** also belongs to this group. Another incredibly useful and high-frequency verb is **працюва́ти**. These verbs describe ongoing actions or regular daily habits. They cleanly tell us what you are doing right now, or what you usually do every single day. This specific concept is known as the imperfective aspect.

> [!culture]
> **Я чита́ю текст. Ми чита́ємо.**
> I read the text. We read.
> The verb **чита́ти** has deep roots in Ukrainian history. In the year 1574, a famous printer named Ivan Fedorov published a beautifully crafted book called the *Apostol* in Lviv. It was the very first printed book in Ukraine. Knowing how to read and write has always been highly valued in traditional Ukrainian culture.

We are starting a new language journey. It will be very interesting and useful.

## Презентація (Presentation)

**Я зна́ю пра́вило. Ми зна́ємо пра́вило. Ти зна́єш все.**
I know the rule. We know the rule. You know everything.

To use a verb in a spoken sentence, you must change its ending. This change tells the listener exactly who is performing the action. Let us look closely at the core pattern for First Conjugation verbs.

Think of the infinitive form as having two separate structural parts: the stem and the ending. For regular verbs like **чита́ти**, the infinitive ending is **-ти**. The stem is everything that comes right before that ending. For this specific word, the stem is **чита-**. 

To conjugate the verb correctly, you drop the **-ти** and add a personal ending. These specific endings always match the personal pronouns.

Here is the complete present tense pattern using the verb **чита́ти**:

| Pronoun | Stem | Ending | Full Verb | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **я** | чита- | **-ю** | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| **ти** | чита- | **-єш** | **ти чита́єш** | you read (singular) |
| **він/вона** | чита- | **-є** | **він/вона чита́є** | he/she reads |
| **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural) |
| **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |

Notice carefully that the regular endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can easily apply it to hundreds of everyday verbs. 

Now we apply this rule. We use the verb "to know". This is a very popular word.

**Я зна́ю все.**
I know everything.

**Він зна́є пра́вду.**
He knows the truth.

**Ми не зна́ємо.**
We do not know.

**Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило.**
They know the rule.

> [!tip]
> **Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш.**
> I understand. You understand.
> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. In Ukrainian, however, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** exclusively means "I". Because of this built-in grammatical information, you can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation.

The verb "to work" has a small change. The part "-юва-" shortens to just "-ю-". But the personal endings remain standard.

**Я працю́ю. Вони́ працю́ють. Ми працю́ємо ра́зом.**
I work. They work. We work together.

**Я працю́ю вдо́ма.**
I work at home.

**Вони́ працю́ють бага́то.**
They work a lot.

These verbs neatly describe continuous processes. The simple present tense smoothly covers both regular habits and current ongoing actions.

## Практика (Practice)

**Я слу́хаю му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ува́жно. Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо.**
I listen to music. You listen carefully. We listen to the radio.

Now it is time for active practice. We are building new sentences. We are describing our daily actions.

Many English speakers make a common structural mistake when they first start using Ukrainian verbs. They try to use the dictionary form directly in their sentences. They mistakenly say the infinitive when they actually mean the conjugated action. This is grammatically incorrect. You must always change the ending to match the person.

Do not use the infinitive form when speaking about actions. You must consistently use the properly conjugated form. 

For the verb "to write", the correct form is different. We speak like this.
**Я пишу́.**
I write.

For the verb "to listen", the correct form uses the standard rule.
**Я слу́хаю.**
I listen.

For the verb "to play", the correct form also uses the standard rule.
**Я гра́ю.**
I play.

Let us practice with a few more common verbs. Pay close attention to how the endings naturally change.

**Вона́ слу́хає ра́діо.**
She listens to the radio.

**Ти ува́жно слу́хаєш.**
You listen carefully.

**Ми слу́хаємо нови́ни.**
We listen to the news.

Another very common habit for English speakers is overusing pronouns. While repeating the pronoun is technically correct and understandable, it sounds highly repetitive. We read the text without extra words. It sounds much better.

**Чита́ю нови́ни. Працю́ю. Відпочива́ю.**
I read the news. I work. I rest.

**Слу́хаю ра́діо. Ду́маю.**
I listen to the radio. I think.

**Пишу́ лист. Чека́ю.**
I write a letter. I wait.

> [!warning]
> **Я пита́ю про це. Він пита́є про це.**
> I ask about this. He asks about this.
> The highly useful verb "to ask" is a notable exception in how it connects to other words in a sentence. Pointing the action at a person requires a different and more complex case rule. For now, only use it safely with the phrase about this.

Let us build simple, complete sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change at all when they become the direct target of an action.

**Я чита́ю журна́л.**
I read a magazine.

**Він пи́ше лист.**
He writes a letter.

**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо.**
We listen to the radio.

**Вони́ гра́ють.**
They play.

You can freely describe your workday. You know the necessary words.

**Я відпочива́ю вдо́ма. Вона́ відпочива́є. Ми чека́ємо.**
I rest at home. She rests. We wait.

**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус.**
We wait for the bus.

**Він чека́є на ві́дповідь.**
He waits for an answer.

**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву.**
You study the language.

**Я вивча́ю слова́.**
I study words.

**Я не розумі́ю.**
I do not understand.

By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву.**
I understand. You understand. We study the language.

**Ми ду́маємо бага́то. Вони́ ду́мають бага́то. Ми працю́ємо бага́то.**
We think a lot. They think a lot. We work a lot.

You learned an important and useful rule. This is a real key to the language. It opens many conversational possibilities.

The First Conjugation pattern is incredibly powerful. Once you thoroughly internalize the regular endings, you can instantly unlock hundreds of verbs. You simply learn the infinitive form, and you immediately know exactly how to use it in a conversation.

You learned how to clearly confidently say what you do. You also learned the vital stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending contains the subject information. 

> [!culture]
> **Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю. А люди́ну — по мо́ві.**
> A bird is known by its feathers. And a person — by their speech.
> There is a famously profound Ukrainian proverb about speaking properly. It broadly means that the way you speak vividly reveals your true character and education. Taking dedicated time to learn correct endings shows deep respect. 

We describe our actions every day. We talk about our habits. This is a big progress.

You are no longer just quietly pointing at static objects. You are actively describing dynamic actions and routines. This represents a truly massive step forward in your learning journey. 

Here are a few useful questions for reviewing the material.

1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun "we" in the First Conjugation? 
Answer: The correct ending is "-ємо". For example:
**Ми чита́ємо.**

2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun "I" in a sentence? 
Answer: No, the verb ending clearly indicates the subject, so you can smoothly drop the pronoun.

3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb "to read"? 
Answer: You drop the infinitive ending to safely get the stem.
**Чита-**

4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? 
**Я чита́ю.**
Or simply:
**Чита́ю.**

 | **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
-| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural/formal) |
+| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural) |
 | **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |
 
-Notice carefully that the endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This specific grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can apply it to hundreds of different verbs in the Ukrainian language without worrying about exceptions. 
+Notice carefully that the regular endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can easily apply it to hundreds of everyday verbs. 
 
-Let us apply the exact same pattern to the verb **зна́ти** (to know). Drop the **-ти** ending to isolate the stem **зна-**.
+Now we apply this rule. We use the verb "to know". This is a very popular word.
 
-**Я зна́ю все (I know everything).**
-**Він зна́є пра́вду (He knows the truth).**
-**Ми не зна́ємо (We do not know).**
-**Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило (They know the rule).**
+**Я зна́ю все.**
+I know everything.
 
+**Він зна́є пра́вду.**
+He knows the truth.
+
+**Ми не зна́ємо.**
+We do not know.
+
+**Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило.**
+They know the rule.
+
 > [!tip]
-> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. You cannot just say "Read a book" if you mean "I read a book." In Ukrainian, however, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** always and exclusively means "I". Because of this built-in grammatical information, you can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and very common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation. 
+> **Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш.**
+> I understand. You understand.
+> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. In Ukrainian, however, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** exclusively means "I". Because of this built-in grammatical information, you can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation.
 
-The important verb **працюва́ти** (to work) also belongs to this group, but it has a slight spelling change. For verbs ending in **-ювати**, the **-юва-** part shortens to **-ю-** before you add the ending. 
+The verb "to work" has a small change. The part "-юва-" shortens to just "-ю-". But the personal endings remain standard.
 
-**Я працю́ю вдо́ма (I work at home).**

+**Я працю́ю. Вони́ працю́ють. Ми працю́ємо ра́зом.**
+I work. They work. We work together.
 
-These verbs describe continuous, ongoing processes. If you say **я працю́ю** (I work / I am working), it means you are engaged in the activity right now, or it is your regular daily habit. Ukrainian does not have a separate continuous tense like "am working" in English. The simple present tense smoothly covers both meanings.
+**Я працю́ю вдо́ма.**
+I work at home.
 
+**Вони́ працю́ють бага́то.**
+They work a lot.

+These verbs neatly describe continuous processes. The simple present tense smoothly covers both regular habits and current ongoing actions.
+
 ## Практика (Practice)
 
-Now it is time to practice building your own sentences. Many English speakers make a very common structural mistake when they first start using Ukrainian verbs. This is grammatically incorrect and sounds confusing to native speakers. You must always change the ending to match the person performing the action.
+**Я слу́хаю му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ува́жно. Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо.**

-Instead of saying **Я гра́ти**, you must say **Я гра́ю** (I play).
-Instead of saying **Я ду́мати**, you must say **Я ду́маю** (I think).

-Let us practice with a few more common verbs. Read these sentences carefully and observe the endings.

 Let us build simple, complete sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change at all when they become the direct target of an action.
 
-**Я чита́ю журна́л (I read a magazine).**
-**Він пи́ше лист (He writes a letter).**
-**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо (We listen to the radio).**

+**Я чита́ю журна́л.**

-You can also use these exact same verbs to clearly describe your daily routine and work activities.
+**Він пи́ше лист.**

-**Я працю́ю над проєктом (I work on a project).**

-**Вони́ ду́мають бага́то (They think a lot).**
+**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо.**
+We listen to the radio.
 
-By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information about your day.
+**Вони́ гра́ють.**
+They play.
 
+You can freely describe your workday. You know the necessary words.
+
+**Я відпочива́ю вдо́ма. Вона́ відпочива́є. Ми чека́ємо.**
+I rest at home. She rests. We wait.
+
+**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус.**
+We wait for the bus.
+
+**Він чека́є на ві́дповідь.**
+He waits for an answer.
+
+**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву.**

+

+
 ## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)
 
-You have learned an incredibly powerful grammatical tool today. The First Conjugation pattern is essentially like a universal master key to the language. Once you thoroughly learn and internalize the **-ю, -єш, -є, -ємо, -єте, -ють** endings, you can instantly unlock and use hundreds of everyday Ukrainian verbs. You simply need to learn the basic infinitive form from the dictionary, and you will immediately know exactly how to use it in a real, functioning sentence.
+**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву.**
+I understand. You understand. We study the language.
 
-You learned how to clearly say what you do: **я чита́ю** (I read), **я працю́ю** (I work), and **я зна́ю** (I know). You also learned the vitally important stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending effectively contains all the necessary subject information within itself. 
+**Ми ду́маємо бага́то. Вони́ ду́мають бага́то. Ми працю́ємо бага́то.**
+We think a lot. They think a lot. We work a lot.
 
+You learned an important and useful rule. This is a real key to the language. It opens many conversational possibilities.
+
+The First Conjugation pattern is incredibly powerful. Once you thoroughly internalize the regular endings, you can instantly unlock hundreds of verbs. You simply learn the infinitive form, and you immediately know exactly how to use it in a conversation.
+
+You learned how to clearly confidently say what you do. You also learned the vital stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending contains the subject information. 
+
 > [!culture]
-> There is a famous, profoundly beautiful Ukrainian proverb: «Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю, а люди́ну по мо́ві» (A bird is known by its feathers, a person by their speech). It broadly means that the way you speak reveals a great deal about your true character, your overall education, and your deep respect for others. Taking the dedicated time to learn and apply the correct grammatical verb endings shows deep respect for the language and genuinely helps you speak much more beautifully and authentically. 
+> **Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю. А люди́ну — по мо́ві.**

-You are no longer just pointing at and naming static objects in a room. You are actively describing ongoing actions, personal habits, and daily routines. This represents a truly massive step forward in your Ukrainian language learning journey. The transition from English instructional scaffolding to real, active Ukrainian language immersion begins with these simple, foundational action statements.
+We describe our actions every day. We talk about our habits. This is a big progress.
 
-Here are a few essential self-check questions to directly help you review and solidify the crucial material from this lesson.
+You are no longer just quietly pointing at static objects. You are actively describing dynamic actions and routines. This represents a truly massive step forward in your learning journey. 
 
-1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun **ми** (we) in the First Conjugation? — The correct and completely regular ending is **-ємо**, for example: **ми чита́ємо** (we read).
-2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun **я** (I) in a sentence to be understood? — No, the verb ending **-ю** clearly indicates the subject on its own, so you can often smoothly drop the pronoun entirely.
-3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb **чита́ти** (to read)? — You drop the **-ти** ending from the standard dictionary infinitive to safely get the stem **чита-**.
-4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? — You can say **Я чита́ю** or, more naturally, simply **Чита́ю**.
+Here are a few useful questions for reviewing the material.
+
+1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun "we" in the First Conjugation? 
+Answer: The correct ending is "-ємо". For example:
+**Ми чита́ємо.**

+2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun "I" in a sentence?

resultDisplay.originalContent:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: First Conjugation (-ати/-яти) in present tense, basic SVO sentences with inanimate objects.
Not covered:
  - Second Conjugation verbs → a1-16
  - Questions and negation → a1-18
-->

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Learning how to conjugate verbs unlocks your ability to describe actions and routines. Instead of just naming static objects around you, you can now confidently say what you do with them every single day. This is the foundation of conversational fluency.

## Вступ (Introduction)

Until now, we have mostly looked at the static world around us. We learned how to identify objects, describe their colors, and point out exactly where they are located. Ми ба́чили ре́чі (We saw objects). Ми зна́ємо слова́ (We know words). We learned how to say what things are. Now, it is time to bring those everyday objects to life with dynamic action.

In this important lesson, you will learn how to describe your daily actions. You will transition from simply saying "this is a book" to proudly stating "I read a book." This means we are finally diving into verbs. A verb is an action word. In the Ukrainian language, verbs are highly dynamic. They change their endings depending on who exactly is doing the action. This fundamental process of changing verb endings is called conjugation. It might seem completely new at first, but it follows very logical and predictable patterns.

You will start with the most common and regular group of verbs. This vast group is often called the First Conjugation. Most of these verbs end in **-ати** in their dictionary form. When you look up an action in a dictionary, you will always find this form. It is called the infinitive. For example, the verb **чита́ти** (to read) is an infinitive. It represents the pure idea of the action before anyone actually performs it. 

You already know many things about this language. **Тепе́р ви зна́єте бі́льше (Now you know more).** The verb **зна́ти** (to know) also belongs to this very same conjugation group. Another incredibly useful and high-frequency verb is **працюва́ти** (to work). These verbs describe ongoing actions or regular habits. They tell us what you are doing right now, or what you usually do every single day. This concept is known as the imperfective aspect.

> [!culture]
> The verb **чита́ти** (to read) has incredibly deep roots in Ukrainian history. In the year 1574, a famous printer named Ivan Fedorov published a book called the *Apostol* in the beautiful city of Lviv. It was the very first printed book in Ukraine. This historic event marked the beginning of a rich, centuries-old literacy tradition. Knowing how to read and write has always been highly valued and deeply respected in traditional Ukrainian culture.

Let us explore exactly how these verbs work in practice. You will soon see that the grammatical pattern is logical, remarkably consistent, and easy to follow.

## Презентація (Presentation)

To use a verb in a real, spoken sentence, you must change its ending. This change tells the listener exactly who is performing the action without needing extra context or explanation. Let us look closely at the core pattern for First Conjugation verbs.

Think of the infinitive form as having two separate structural parts: the stem and the ending. For regular verbs like **чита́ти** (to read), the infinitive ending is **-ти**. The stem is everything that comes right before that ending. For this specific word, the stem is **чита-**. 

To conjugate the verb correctly, you drop the **-ти** and add a personal ending. These personal endings always match the pronouns: **я** (I), **ти** (you singular), **він/вона/воно** (he/she/it), **ми** (we), **ви** (you plural/formal), and **вони** (they).

Here is the complete present tense pattern using the verb **чита́ти** (to read):

| Pronoun | Stem | Ending | Full Verb | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **я** | чита- | **-ю** | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| **ти** | чита- | **-єш** | **ти чита́єш** | you read (singular) |
| **він/вона** | чита- | **-є** | **він/вона чита́є** | he/she reads |
| **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural/formal) |
| **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |

Notice carefully that the endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This specific grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can apply it to hundreds of different verbs in the Ukrainian language without worrying about exceptions. 

Let us apply the exact same pattern to the verb **зна́ти** (to know). Drop the **-ти** ending to isolate the stem **зна-**.

**Я зна́ю все (I know everything).**
**Він зна́є пра́вду (He knows the truth).**
**Ми не зна́ємо (We do not know).**
**Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило (They know the rule).**

> [!tip]
> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. You cannot just say "Read a book" if you mean "I read a book." In Ukrainian, however, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** always and exclusively means "I". Because of this built-in grammatical information, you can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and very common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation. 

The important verb **працюва́ти** (to work) also belongs to this group, but it has a slight spelling change. For verbs ending in **-ювати**, the **-юва-** part shortens to **-ю-** before you add the ending. 

**Я працю́ю вдо́ма (I work at home).**
**Вони́ працю́ють бага́то (They work a lot).**
**Ми працю́ємо ра́зом (We work together).**

These verbs describe continuous, ongoing processes. If you say **я працю́ю** (I work / I am working), it means you are engaged in the activity right now, or it is your regular daily habit. Ukrainian does not have a separate continuous tense like "am working" in English. The simple present tense smoothly covers both meanings.

## Практика (Practice)

Now it is time to practice building your own sentences. Many English speakers make a very common structural mistake when they first start using Ukrainian verbs. They try to use the dictionary form, the infinitive, directly in their sentences. They might mistakenly say **Я чита́ти** when they actually mean "I read." This is grammatically incorrect and sounds confusing to native speakers. You must always change the ending to match the person performing the action.

Remember to constantly replace the incorrect infinitive with the properly conjugated form. 
Instead of saying **Я писа́ти**, you must say **Я пишу́** (I write).
Instead of saying **Я слу́хати**, you must say **Я слу́хаю** (I listen).
Instead of saying **Я гра́ти**, you must say **Я гра́ю** (I play).
Instead of saying **Я ду́мати**, you must say **Я ду́маю** (I think).

Let us practice with a few more common verbs. Read these sentences carefully and observe the endings.

**Я слу́хаю му́зику (I listen to music).**
**Вона́ слу́хає ра́діо (She listens to the radio).**
**Ти ува́жно слу́хаєш (You listen carefully).**
**Ми слу́хаємо нови́ни (We listen to the news).**

Another very common habit for English speakers is overusing pronouns. Because strict English grammar requires "I", "you", or "we" in nearly every single sentence, you might be tempted to say **Я чита́ю. Я працю́ю. Я зна́ю.** While this is technically correct and completely understandable, it sounds highly repetitive and slightly unnatural in casual Ukrainian conversation.

Try reading these short descriptive sequences without the constant repetition of "я". 

**Чита́ю нови́ни. Працю́ю. Відпочива́ю. (I read the news. I work. I rest.)**
**Слу́хаю ра́діо. Ду́маю. (I listen to the radio. I think.)**
**Пишу́ лист. Чека́ю. (I write a letter. I wait.)**

Notice how much smoother and more native it sounds. The verb endings do all the heavy lifting for you, making your speech sound flowing and confident.

> [!warning]
> The highly useful verb **пита́ти** (to ask) is a notable exception in how it connects to other words in a sentence. Do not use it to say "ask someone" just yet, because pointing the action at a person requires a different and more complex case rule. For now, only use it safely with the phrase **про це** (about this).
> **Я пита́ю про це (I ask about this).**
> **Він пита́є про це (He asks about this).**

Let us build simple, complete sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change at all when they become the direct target of an action.

**Я чита́ю журна́л (I read a magazine).**
**Він пи́ше лист (He writes a letter).**
**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо (We listen to the radio).**
**Вони́ гра́ють у го́льф (They play golf).**

You can also use these exact same verbs to clearly describe your daily routine and work activities.

**Я працю́ю над проєктом (I work on a project).**
**По́тім я відпочива́ю вдо́ма (Then I rest at home).**
**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус (We wait for the bus).**
**Він чека́є на ві́дповідь (He waits for an answer).**
**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву (You study the language).**
**Я вивча́ю слова́ (I study words).**
**Я не розумі́ю (I do not understand).**
**Вони́ ду́мають бага́то (They think a lot).**

By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information about your day.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

You have learned an incredibly powerful grammatical tool today. The First Conjugation pattern is essentially like a universal master key to the language. Once you thoroughly learn and internalize the **-ю, -єш, -є, -ємо, -єте, -ють** endings, you can instantly unlock and use hundreds of everyday Ukrainian verbs. You simply need to learn the basic infinitive form from the dictionary, and you will immediately know exactly how to use it in a real, functioning sentence.

You learned how to clearly say what you do: **я чита́ю** (I read), **я працю́ю** (I work), and **я зна́ю** (I know). You also learned the vitally important stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending effectively contains all the necessary subject information within itself. 

> [!culture]
> There is a famous, profoundly beautiful Ukrainian proverb: «Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю, а люди́ну по мо́ві» (A bird is known by its feathers, a person by their speech). It broadly means that the way you speak reveals a great deal about your true character, your overall education, and your deep respect for others. Taking the dedicated time to learn and apply the correct grammatical verb endings shows deep respect for the language and genuinely helps you speak much more beautifully and authentically. 

You are no longer just pointing at and naming static objects in a room. You are actively describing ongoing actions, personal habits, and daily routines. This represents a truly massive step forward in your Ukrainian language learning journey. The transition from English instructional scaffolding to real, active Ukrainian language immersion begins with these simple, foundational action statements.

Here are a few essential self-check questions to directly help you review and solidify the crucial material from this lesson.

1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun **ми** (we) in the First Conjugation? — The correct and completely regular ending is **-ємо**, for example: **ми чита́ємо** (we read).
2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun **я** (I) in a sentence to be understood? — No, the verb ending **-ю** clearly indicates the subject on its own, so you can often smoothly drop the pronoun entirely.
3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb **чита́ти** (to read)? — You drop the **-ти** ending from the standard dictionary infinitive to safely get the stem **чита-**.
4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? — You can say **Я чита́ю** or, more naturally, simply **Чита́ю**.

<!-- SCOPE
Covers: First Conjugation (-ати/-яти) in present tense, basic SVO sentences with inanimate objects.
Not covered:
  - Second Conjugation verbs → a1-16
  - Questions and negation → a1-18
-->

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Learning how to conjugate verbs unlocks your ability to describe actions and routines. Instead of simply naming static objects around you, you can now confidently say what you do with them every single day. This is the foundation of conversational fluency.

## Вступ (Introduction)

**Ми вивча́ємо слова́. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Тепе́р ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
We study words. We study the language. Now we know a lot.

**Я чита́ю кни́гу. Ти чита́єш нови́ни.**
I read a book. You read the news.

In this important lesson, you will learn how to describe your daily actions and habits. You will transition from simply naming objects to proudly stating what you actively do with them. A verb is an action word. In the Ukrainian language, verbs change their endings depending on who exactly is doing the action. This fundamental process of changing verb endings is called conjugation. It follows very logical and predictable patterns.

You will start with the most common and regular group of verbs. This large group is called the First Conjugation. Most of these verbs end in **-ати** in their dictionary form. This base form is called the infinitive. For example, the verb **чита́ти** is an infinitive. 

The verb **зна́ти** also belongs to this group. Another incredibly useful and high-frequency verb is **працюва́ти**. These verbs describe ongoing actions or regular daily habits. They cleanly tell us what you are doing right now, or what you usually do every single day. This specific concept is known as the imperfective aspect.

> [!culture]
> **Я чита́ю текст. Ми чита́ємо.**
> I read the text. We read.
> The verb **чита́ти** has deep roots in Ukrainian history. In the year 1574, a famous printer named Ivan Fedorov published a beautifully crafted book called the *Apostol* in Lviv. It was the very first printed book in Ukraine. Knowing how to read and write has always been highly valued in traditional Ukrainian culture.

We are starting a new language journey. It will be very interesting and useful.

## Презентація (Presentation)

**Я зна́ю пра́вило. Ми зна́ємо пра́вило. Ти зна́єш все.**
I know the rule. We know the rule. You know everything.

To use a verb in a spoken sentence, you must change its ending. This change tells the listener exactly who is performing the action. Let us look closely at the core pattern for First Conjugation verbs.

Think of the infinitive form as having two separate structural parts: the stem and the ending. For regular verbs like **чита́ти**, the infinitive ending is **-ти**. The stem is everything that comes right before that ending. For this specific word, the stem is **чита-**. 

To conjugate the verb correctly, you drop the **-ти** and add a personal ending. These specific endings always match the personal pronouns.

Here is the complete present tense pattern using the verb **чита́ти**:

| Pronoun | Stem | Ending | Full Verb | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **я** | чита- | **-ю** | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| **ти** | чита- | **-єш** | **ти чита́єш** | you read (singular) |
| **він/вона** | чита- | **-є** | **він/вона чита́є** | he/she reads |
| **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural) |
| **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |

Notice carefully that the regular endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can easily apply it to hundreds of everyday verbs. 

Now we apply this rule. We use the verb "to know". This is a very popular word.

**Я зна́ю все.**
I know everything.

**Він зна́є пра́вду.**
He knows the truth.

**Ми не зна́ємо.**
We do not know.

**Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило.**
They know the rule.

> [!tip]
> **Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш.**
> I understand. You understand.
> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. In Ukrainian, however, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** exclusively means "I". Because of this built-in grammatical information, you can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation.

The verb "to work" has a small change. The part "-юва-" shortens to just "-ю-". But the personal endings remain standard.

**Я працю́ю. Вони́ працю́ють. Ми працю́ємо ра́зом.**
I work. They work. We work together.

**Я працю́ю вдо́ма.**
I work at home.

**Вони́ працю́ють бага́то.**
They work a lot.

These verbs neatly describe continuous processes. The simple present tense smoothly covers both regular habits and current ongoing actions.

## Практика (Practice)

**Я слу́хаю му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ува́жно. Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо.**
I listen to music. You listen carefully. We listen to the radio.

Now it is time for active practice. We are building new sentences. We are describing our daily actions.

Many English speakers make a common structural mistake when they first start using Ukrainian verbs. They try to use the dictionary form directly in their sentences. They mistakenly say the infinitive when they actually mean the conjugated action. This is grammatically incorrect. You must always change the ending to match the person.

Do not use the infinitive form when speaking about actions. You must consistently use the properly conjugated form. 

For the verb "to write", the correct form is different. We speak like this.
**Я пишу́.**
I write.

For the verb "to listen", the correct form uses the standard rule.
**Я слу́хаю.**
I listen.

For the verb "to play", the correct form also uses the standard rule.
**Я гра́ю.**
I play.

Let us practice with a few more common verbs. Pay close attention to how the endings naturally change.

**Вона́ слу́хає ра́діо.**
She listens to the radio.

**Ти ува́жно слу́хаєш.**
You listen carefully.

**Ми слу́хаємо нови́ни.**
We listen to the news.

Another very common habit for English speakers is overusing pronouns. While repeating the pronoun is technically correct and understandable, it sounds highly repetitive. We read the text without extra words. It sounds much better.

**Чита́ю нови́ни. Працю́ю. Відпочива́ю.**
I read the news. I work. I rest.

**Слу́хаю ра́діо. Ду́маю.**
I listen to the radio. I think.

**Пишу́ лист. Чека́ю.**
I write a letter. I wait.

> [!warning]
> **Я пита́ю про це. Він пита́є про це.**
> I ask about this. He asks about this.
> The highly useful verb "to ask" is a notable exception in how it connects to other words in a sentence. Pointing the action at a person requires a different and more complex case rule. For now, only use it safely with the phrase about this.

Let us build simple, complete sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change at all when they become the direct target of an action.

**Я чита́ю журна́л.**
I read a magazine.

**Він пи́ше лист.**
He writes a letter.

**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо.**
We listen to the radio.

**Вони́ гра́ють.**
They play.

You can freely describe your workday. You know the necessary words.

**Я відпочива́ю вдо́ма. Вона́ відпочива́є. Ми чека́ємо.**
I rest at home. She rests. We wait.

**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус.**
We wait for the bus.

**Він чека́є на ві́дповідь.**
He waits for an answer.

**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву.**
You study the language.

**Я вивча́ю слова́.**
I study words.

**Я не розумі́ю.**
I do not understand.

By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву.**
I understand. You understand. We study the language.

**Ми ду́маємо бага́то. Вони́ ду́мають бага́то. Ми працю́ємо бага́то.**
We think a lot. They think a lot. We work a lot.

You learned an important and useful rule. This is a real key to the language. It opens many conversational possibilities.

The First Conjugation pattern is incredibly powerful. Once you thoroughly internalize the regular endings, you can instantly unlock hundreds of verbs. You simply learn the infinitive form, and you immediately know exactly how to use it in a conversation.

You learned how to clearly confidently say what you do. You also learned the vital stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending contains the subject information. 

> [!culture]
> **Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю. А люди́ну — по мо́ві.**
> A bird is known by its feathers. And a person — by their speech.
> There is a famously profound Ukrainian proverb about speaking properly. It broadly means that the way you speak vividly reveals your true character and education. Taking dedicated time to learn correct endings shows deep respect. 

We describe our actions every day. We talk about our habits. This is a big progress.

You are no longer just quietly pointing at static objects. You are actively describing dynamic actions and routines. This represents a truly massive step forward in your learning journey. 

Here are a few useful questions for reviewing the material.

1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun "we" in the First Conjugation? 
Answer: The correct ending is "-ємо". For example:
**Ми чита́ємо.**

2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun "I" in a sentence? 
Answer: No, the verb ending clearly indicates the subject, so you can smoothly drop the pronoun.

3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb "to read"? 
Answer: You drop the infinitive ending to safely get the stem.
**Чита-**

4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? 
**Я чита́ю.**
Or simply:
**Чита́ю.**

This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.

**Я чита́ю кни́гу. Ти чита́єш нови́ни. Вона́ чита́є текст.**

## Презентація (Presentation)

**Я зна́ю пра́вило. Ми зна́ємо пра́вило. Ти зна́єш все. Вони́ зна́ють.**

To use a verb in a spoken sentence, you must change its ending. This change tells the listener exactly who is performing the action. Let us look closely at the core pattern for First Conjugation verbs.

Here is the complete present tense pattern using the verb **чита́ти**:

| Pronoun | Stem | Ending | Full Verb | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **я** | чита- | **-ю** | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| **ти** | чита- | **-єш** | **ти чита́єш** | you read (singular) |
| **він/вона** | чита- | **-є** | **він/вона чита́є** | he/she reads |
| **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural) |
| **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |
