Now we apply this rule. We use the verb "to know". This is a very popular word.

**Я зна́ю все. Він зна́є пра́вду. Ми не зна́ємо. Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило.**
I know everything. He knows the truth. We do not know. They know the rule.

> [!tip]
> **Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Він розумі́є.**
> I understand. You understand. He understands.
> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. In Ukrainian, however, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** exclusively means "I". Because of this built-in grammatical information, you can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation.

The verb "to work" has a small change. The part "-юва-" shortens to just "-ю-". But the personal endings remain standard.

**Я працю́ю. Ти працю́єш. Вона́ працю́є.**
I work. You work. She works.

**Ми працю́ємо ра́зом. Вони́ працю́ють бага́то.**
We work together. They work a lot.

These verbs neatly describe continuous processes. The simple present tense smoothly covers both regular habits and current ongoing actions.

## Практика (Practice)

**Я слу́хаю му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ува́жно. Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
I listen to music. You listen carefully. We listen to the radio. They listen.

Now it is time for active practice. We are building new sentences. We are describing our daily actions using our target verbs. 

Many English speakers make a common structural mistake when they first start using Ukrainian verbs. They mistakenly say the infinitive when they actually mean the conjugated action. This is grammatically incorrect. You must always change the ending to match the person.

Do not use the infinitive form when speaking about actions. You must consistently use the properly conjugated form. 

When using the verb "to write", the correct form is slightly different. We speak like this.
**Я пишу́. Він пи́ше. Ми пи́шемо.**
I write. He writes. We write.

Looking at the verb "to listen", the correct form uses the standard rule.
**Я слу́хаю. Вона́ слу́хає. Вони́ слу́хають.**
I listen. She listens. They listen.

The verb "to play" also uses the standard rule perfectly.
**Я гра́ю. Ти гра́єш. Ми гра́ємо.**
I play. You play. We play.

Let us practice with a few more common verbs. Pay close attention to how the endings naturally change.

Another very common habit for English speakers is overusing pronouns. While repeating the pronoun is technically correct and understandable, it sounds highly repetitive. We read the text without extra words. It sounds much better.

**Чита́ю нови́ни. Працю́ю. Відпочива́ю.**
I read the news. I work. I rest.

**Слу́хаю ра́діо. Ду́маю. Чека́ю.**
I listen to the radio. I think. I wait.

> [!warning]
> **Я пита́ю про це. Він пита́є про це. Ми пита́ємо.**
> I ask about this. He asks about this. We ask.
> The highly useful verb "to ask" is a notable exception in how it connects to other words in a sentence. Pointing the action at a person requires a different and more complex case rule. For now, only use it safely with the phrase "about this".

Let us build simple, complete sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change at all when they become the direct target of an action.

**Я чита́ю журна́л. Ми чита́ємо текст.**
I read a magazine. We read the text.

**Він пи́ше лист. Вони́ пи́шуть.**
He writes a letter. They write.

**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вона́ слу́хає.**
We listen to the radio. She listens.

You can freely describe your workday. You know the necessary words to explain your full routine. Let us review the patterns in action.

**Я працю́ю бага́то. Ти працю́єш.**
I work a lot. You work.

**Я відпочива́ю вдо́ма. Вона́ відпочива́є. Ми відпочива́ємо.**
I rest at home. She rests. We rest.

**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус. Він чека́є.**
We wait for the bus. He waits.

**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву. Ми вивча́ємо слова́.**
You study the language. We study words.

**Я не розумі́ю. Вона́ розумі́є все.**
I do not understand. She understands everything.

By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Вони́ вивча́ють.**
I understand. You understand. We study the language. They study.

**Ми ду́маємо бага́то. Вони́ ду́мають бага́то. Ти ду́маєш.**
We think a lot. They think a lot. You think.

You learned an important and useful rule today. This is a real key to the language. It opens many conversational possibilities.

The First Conjugation pattern is incredibly powerful. Once you thoroughly internalize the regular endings, you can instantly unlock hundreds of verbs. You simply learn the infinitive form, and you immediately know exactly how to use it in a conversation.

You learned how to clearly confidently say what you do. You also learned the vital stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending contains the subject information. 

> [!culture]
> **Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю. А люди́ну — по мо́ві.**
> A bird is known by its feathers. And a person — by their speech.
> There is a famously profound Ukrainian proverb about speaking properly. It broadly means that the way you speak vividly reveals your true character and education. Taking dedicated time to learn correct endings shows deep respect. 

We describe our actions every day. We talk about our habits. This is a big progress.

You are no longer just quietly pointing at static objects. You are actively describing dynamic actions and routines. This represents a truly massive step forward in your learning journey. 

Here are a few useful questions for reviewing the material.

1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun "we" in the First Conjugation? 
Answer: The correct ending is "-ємо". For example:
**Ми чита́ємо.**

2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun "I" in a sentence? 
Answer: No, the verb ending clearly indicates the subject, so you can smoothly drop the pronoun.

3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb "to read"? 
Answer: You drop the infinitive ending to safely get the stem.
**Чита-**

4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? 
**Я чита́ю.**
Or simply:
**Чита́ю.**

In the Ukrainian language, verbs change their endings depending on who exactly is doing the action. This fundamental process of changing verb endings is called conjugation. It follows very logical and predictable patterns.

You will start with the most common and regular group of verbs. This large group is called the First Conjugation. Most of these verbs end in **-ати** in their dictionary form. This base form is called the infinitive. For example, the verb **чита́ти** is an infinitive.

<!-- SCOPE

Not covered:
  - Second Conjugation verbs → a1-16
  - Questions and negation → a1-18
-->

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Learning how to conjugate verbs unlocks your ability to describe actions and routines. Instead of simply naming static objects around you, you can now confidently say what you do with them every single day. This is the foundation of conversational fluency.

## Вступ (Introduction)

**Ми вивча́ємо слова́. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Тепе́р ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
We study words. We study the language. Now we know a lot.

**Я чита́ю кни́гу. Ти чита́єш нови́ни.**
I read a book. You read the news.

In this important lesson, you will learn how to describe your daily actions and habits. You will transition from simply naming objects to proudly stating what you actively do with them. A verb is an action word. In the Ukrainian language, verbs change their endings depending on who exactly is doing the action. This fundamental process of changing verb endings is called conjugation. It follows very logical and predictable patterns.

You will start with the most common and regular group of verbs. This large group is called the First Conjugation. Most of these verbs end in **-ати** in their dictionary form. This base form is called the infinitive. For example, the verb **чита́ти** is an infinitive. 

The verb **зна́ти** also belongs to this group. Another incredibly useful and high-frequency verb is **працюва́ти**. These verbs describe ongoing actions or regular daily habits. They cleanly tell us what you are doing right now, or what you usually do every single day. This specific concept is known as the imperfective aspect.

> [!culture]
> **Я чита́ю текст. Ми чита́ємо.**
> I read the text. We read.
> The verb **чита́ти** has deep roots in Ukrainian history. In the year 1574, a famous printer named Ivan Fedorov published a beautifully crafted book called the *Apostol* in Lviv. It was the very first printed book in Ukraine. Knowing how to read and write has always been highly valued in traditional Ukrainian culture.

We are starting a new language journey. It will be very interesting and useful.

## Презентація (Presentation)

**Я зна́ю пра́вило. Ми зна́ємо пра́вило. Ти зна́єш все.**
I know the rule. We know the rule. You know everything.

To use a verb in a spoken sentence, you must change its ending. This change tells the listener exactly who is performing the action. Let us look closely at the core pattern for First Conjugation verbs.

Think of the infinitive form as having two separate structural parts: the stem and the ending. For regular verbs like **чита́ти**, the infinitive ending is **-ти**. The stem is everything that comes right before that ending. For this specific word, the stem is **чита-**. 

To conjugate the verb correctly, you drop the **-ти** and add a personal ending. These specific endings always match the personal pronouns.

Here is the complete present tense pattern using the verb **чита́ти**:

| Pronoun | Stem | Ending | Full Verb | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **я** | чита- | **-ю** | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| **ти** | чита- | **-єш** | **ти чита́єш** | you read (singular) |
| **він/вона** | чита- | **-є** | **він/вона чита́є** | he/she reads |
| **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural) |
| **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |

Notice carefully that the regular endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can easily apply it to hundreds of everyday verbs. 

Now we apply this rule. We use the verb "to know". This is a very popular word.

**Я зна́ю все.**
I know everything.

**Він зна́є пра́вду.**
He knows the truth.

**Ми не зна́ємо.**
We do not know.

**Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило.**
They know the rule.

> [!tip]
> **Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш.**
> I understand. You understand.
> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. In Ukrainian, however, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** exclusively means "I". Because of this built-in grammatical information, you can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation.

The verb "to work" has a small change. The part "-юва-" shortens to just "-ю-". But the personal endings remain standard.

**Я працю́ю. Вони́ працю́ють. Ми працю́ємо ра́зом.**
I work. They work. We work together.

**Я працю́ю вдо́ма.**
I work at home.

**Вони́ працю́ють бага́то.**
They work a lot.

These verbs neatly describe continuous processes. The simple present tense smoothly covers both regular habits and current ongoing actions.

## Практика (Practice)

**Я слу́хаю му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ува́жно. Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо.**
I listen to music. You listen carefully. We listen to the radio.

Now it is time for active practice. We are building new sentences. We are describing our daily actions.

Many English speakers make a common structural mistake when they first start using Ukrainian verbs. They try to use the dictionary form directly in their sentences. They mistakenly say the infinitive when they actually mean the conjugated action. This is grammatically incorrect. You must always change the ending to match the person.

Do not use the infinitive form when speaking about actions. You must consistently use the properly conjugated form. 

For the verb "to write", the correct form is different. We speak like this.
**Я пишу́.**
I write.

For the verb "to listen", the correct form uses the standard rule.
**Я слу́хаю.**
I listen.

For the verb "to play", the correct form also uses the standard rule.
**Я гра́ю.**
I play.

Let us practice with a few more common verbs. Pay close attention to how the endings naturally change.

**Вона́ слу́хає ра́діо.**
She listens to the radio.

**Ти ува́жно слу́хаєш.**
You listen carefully.

**Ми слу́хаємо нови́ни.**
We listen to the news.

Another very common habit for English speakers is overusing pronouns. While repeating the pronoun is technically correct and understandable, it sounds highly repetitive. We read the text without extra words. It sounds much better.

**Чита́ю нови́ни. Працю́ю. Відпочива́ю.**
I read the news. I work. I rest.

**Слу́хаю ра́діо. Ду́маю.**
I listen to the radio. I think.

**Пишу́ лист. Чека́ю.**
I write a letter. I wait.

> [!warning]
> **Я пита́ю про це. Він пита́є про це.**
> I ask about this. He asks about this.
> The highly useful verb "to ask" is a notable exception in how it connects to other words in a sentence. Pointing the action at a person requires a different and more complex case rule. For now, only use it safely with the phrase about this.

Let us build simple, complete sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change at all when they become the direct target of an action.

**Я чита́ю журна́л.**
I read a magazine.

**Він пи́ше лист.**
He writes a letter.

**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо.**
We listen to the radio.

**Вони́ гра́ють.**
They play.

You can freely describe your workday. You know the necessary words.

**Я відпочива́ю вдо́ма. Вона́ відпочива́є. Ми чека́ємо.**
I rest at home. She rests. We wait.

**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус.**
We wait for the bus.

**Він чека́є на ві́дповідь.**
He waits for an answer.

**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву.**
You study the language.

**Я вивча́ю слова́.**
I study words.

**Я не розумі́ю.**
I do not understand.

By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву.**
I understand. You understand. We study the language.

**Ми ду́маємо бага́то. Вони́ ду́мають бага́то. Ми працю́ємо бага́то.**
We think a lot. They think a lot. We work a lot.

You learned an important and useful rule. This is a real key to the language. It opens many conversational possibilities.

The First Conjugation pattern is incredibly powerful. Once you thoroughly internalize the regular endings, you can instantly unlock hundreds of verbs. You simply learn the infinitive form, and you immediately know exactly how to use it in a conversation.

You learned how to clearly confidently say what you do. You also learned the vital stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending contains the subject information. 

> [!culture]
> **Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю. А люди́ну — по мо́ві.**
> A bird is known by its feathers. And a person — by their speech.
> There is a famously profound Ukrainian proverb about speaking properly. It broadly means that the way you speak vividly reveals your true character and education. Taking dedicated time to learn correct endings shows deep respect. 

We describe our actions every day. We talk about our habits. This is a big progress.

You are no longer just quietly pointing at static objects. You are actively describing dynamic actions and routines. This represents a truly massive step forward in your learning journey. 

Here are a few useful questions for reviewing the material.

1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun "we" in the First Conjugation? 
Answer: The correct ending is "-ємо". For example:
**Ми чита́ємо.**

2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun "I" in a sentence? 
Answer: No, the verb ending clearly indicates the subject, so you can smoothly drop the pronoun.

3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb "to read"? 
Answer: You drop the infinitive ending to safely get the stem.
**Чита-**

4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? 
**Я чита́ю.**
Or simply:
**Чита́ю.**

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.

args.content:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: First Conjugation (-ати/-яти) in present tense, basic SVO sentences with inanimate objects.
Not covered:
  - Second Conjugation verbs → a1-16
  - Questions and negation → a1-18
-->

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Learning how to conjugate verbs unlocks your ability to describe actions and routines. Instead of simply naming static objects around you, you can now confidently say what you do with them every single day. This is the foundation of conversational fluency.

## Вступ (Introduction)

**Ми вивча́ємо слова́. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Тепе́р ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
We study words. We study the language. Now we know a lot.

**Я чита́ю кни́гу. Ти чита́єш нови́ни. Вона́ чита́є текст. Ми чита́ємо.**
I read a book. You read the news. She reads the text. We read.

In this important lesson, you will learn how to describe your daily actions. You will transition from simply naming objects to proudly stating what you actively do with them. A verb is an action word. In the Ukrainian language, verbs change their endings depending on who exactly is doing the action. This fundamental process of changing verb endings is called conjugation. It follows very logical and predictable patterns.

You will start with the most common and regular group of verbs. This large group is called the First Conjugation. Most of these verbs end in **-ати** in their dictionary form. This base form is called the infinitive. For example, the verb **чита́ти** is an infinitive. 

The verb **зна́ти** also belongs to this group. Another incredibly useful and high-frequency verb is **працюва́ти**. These verbs describe ongoing actions or regular daily habits. They cleanly tell us what you are doing right now, or what you usually do every single day. This specific concept is known as the imperfective aspect.

> [!culture]
> **Я чита́ю текст. Ми чита́ємо. Вони́ чита́ють.**
> I read the text. We read. They read.
> The verb **чита́ти** has deep roots in Ukrainian history. In the year 1574, a famous printer named Ivan Fedorov published a beautifully crafted book called the *Apostol* in Lviv. It was the very first printed book in Ukraine. Knowing how to read and write has always been highly valued in traditional Ukrainian culture.

We are starting a new language journey. It will be very interesting and useful.

## Презентація (Presentation)

**Я зна́ю пра́вило. Ми зна́ємо пра́вило. Ти зна́єш все. Вони́ зна́ють.**
I know the rule. We know the rule. You know everything. They know.

To use a verb in a spoken sentence, you must change its ending. This change tells the listener exactly who is performing the action. Let us look closely at the core pattern for First Conjugation verbs.

Think of the infinitive form as having two separate structural parts: the stem and the ending. For regular verbs like **чита́ти**, the infinitive ending is **-ти**. The stem is everything that comes right before that ending. For this specific word, the stem is **чита-**. 

To conjugate the verb correctly, you drop the **-ти** and add a personal ending. These specific endings always match the personal pronouns.

Here is the complete present tense pattern using the verb **чита́ти**:

| Pronoun | Stem | Ending | Full Verb | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **я** | чита- | **-ю** | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| **ти** | чита- | **-єш** | **ти чита́єш** | you read (singular) |
| **він/вона** | чита- | **-є** | **він/вона чита́є** | he/she reads |
| **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural) |
| **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |

Notice carefully that the regular endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can easily apply it to hundreds of everyday verbs. 

Now we apply this rule. We use the verb "to know". This is a very popular word.

**Я зна́ю все. Він зна́є пра́вду. Ми не зна́ємо. Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило.**
I know everything. He knows the truth. We do not know. They know the rule.

> [!tip]
> **Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Він розумі́є. Ми розумі́ємо.**
> I understand. You understand. He understands. We understand.
> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. In Ukrainian, however, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** exclusively means "I". Because of this built-in grammatical information, you can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation.

The verb "to work" has a small spelling change. The part "-юва-" shortens to just "-ю-". But the personal endings remain standard.

**Я працю́ю. Ти працю́єш. Вона́ працю́є.**
I work. You work. She works.

**Ми працю́ємо ра́зом. Вони́ працю́ють бага́то.**
We work together. They work a lot.

**Ми вивча́ємо слова́. Це нове́ пра́вило.**
We study words. This is a new rule.

**Він працю́є вдо́ма. Вони́ працю́ють.**
He works at home. They work.

These verbs neatly describe continuous processes. The simple present tense smoothly covers both regular habits and current ongoing actions. You do not need a separate tense for actions happening right now.

## Практика (Practice)

**Я слу́хаю му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ува́жно. Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
I listen to music. You listen carefully. We listen to the radio. They listen.

Now it is time for active practice. We are building new sentences. We are describing our daily actions using our target verbs. 

Many English speakers make a common structural mistake when they first start using Ukrainian verbs. They mistakenly say the infinitive when they actually mean the conjugated action. This is grammatically incorrect. You must always change the ending to match the person.

Do not use the infinitive form when speaking about actions. You must consistently use the properly conjugated form. 

When using the verb "to write", the correct form is slightly different. We speak like this.
**Я пишу́. Він пи́ше. Ми пи́шемо. Вони́ пи́шуть.**
I write. He writes. We write. They write.

Looking at the verb "to listen", the correct form uses the standard rule.
**Я слу́хаю. Вона́ слу́хає. Ми слу́хаємо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
I listen. She listens. We listen. They listen.

The verb "to play" also uses the standard rule perfectly.
**Я гра́ю. Ти гра́єш. Ми гра́ємо. Вони́ гра́ють.**
I play. You play. We play. They play.

Another very common habit for English speakers is overusing pronouns. While repeating the pronoun is technically correct and understandable, it sounds highly repetitive. We read the text without extra words. It sounds much better.

**Чита́ю нови́ни. Працю́ю. Відпочива́ю.**
I read the news. I work. I rest.

**Слу́хаю ра́діо. Ду́маю. Чека́ю.**
I listen to the radio. I think. I wait.

**Пишу́ лист. Чека́ю. Гра́ю.**
I write a letter. I wait. I play.

> [!warning]
> **Я пита́ю про це. Він пита́є про це. Ми пита́ємо.**
> I ask about this. He asks about this. We ask.
> The highly useful verb "to ask" is a notable exception in how it connects to other words in a sentence. Pointing the action at a person requires a different and more complex case rule. For now, only use it safely with the exact phrase "about this".

Let us build simple, complete sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change at all when they become the direct target of an action.

**Я чита́ю журна́л. Ми чита́ємо текст. Вона́ чита́є.**
I read a magazine. We read the text. She reads.

**Він пи́ше лист. Вони́ пи́шуть. Ти пи́шеш.**
He writes a letter. They write. You write.

**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вона́ слу́хає му́зику.**
We listen to the radio. She listens to music.

You can freely describe your workday. You know the necessary words to explain your full routine. Let us carefully review the patterns in a short reading block.

**Мій день.**
**Я працю́ю вдо́ма. Я працю́ю бага́то. Ми працю́ємо ра́зом.**
**Вона́ слу́хає му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ра́діо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
**Я чита́ю нови́ни. Він чита́є текст. Ми чита́ємо.**
**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву. Я вивча́ю слова́. Ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
**Я ду́маю. Ти розумі́єш. Вона́ зна́є пра́вду.**
**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус. Вони́ чека́ють.**
**По́тім ми відпочива́ємо вдо́ма. Я гра́ю. Вони́ гра́ють.**

My day.
I work at home. I work a lot. We work together.
She listens to music. You listen to the radio. They listen.
I read the news. He reads the text. We read.
You study the language. I study words. We know a lot.
I think. You understand. She knows the truth.
We wait for the bus. They wait.
Then we rest at home. I play. They play.

By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Вони́ вивча́ють.**
I understand. You understand. We study the language. They study.

**Ми ду́маємо бага́то. Вони́ ду́мають бага́то. Ти ду́маєш.**
We think a lot. They think a lot. You think.

You learned an important and highly useful rule today. This is a real foundational key to the language. It safely opens many new conversational possibilities.

The First Conjugation pattern is incredibly powerful. Once you thoroughly internalize the regular endings, you can instantly unlock hundreds of new verbs. You simply learn the dictionary infinitive form, and you immediately know exactly how to correctly use it in a conversation.

You learned how to clearly and confidently say what you do. You also learned the vital stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending effectively contains the subject information. 

> [!culture]
> **Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю. А люди́ну — по мо́ві.**
> A bird is known by its feathers. And a person — by their speech.
> There is a famously profound Ukrainian proverb about speaking properly. It broadly means that the way you speak vividly reveals your true character and education. Taking dedicated time to accurately learn correct endings shows deep respect for the language. 

We describe our daily actions every day. We actively talk about our regular habits. This is a big sign of clear progress.

You are no longer just quietly pointing at static objects in the room. You are actively describing dynamic actions and daily routines. This undeniably represents a truly massive step forward in your language learning journey. 

Here are a few useful practical questions for thoroughly reviewing the material.

1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun "we" in the First Conjugation? 
Answer: The correct ending is "-ємо". For example:
**Ми чита́ємо.**

2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun "I" in a sentence? 
Answer: No, the verb ending clearly indicates the exact subject, so you can smoothly drop the pronoun.

3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb "to read"? 
Answer: You drop the standard infinitive ending to safely get the stem.
**Чита-**

4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? 
**Я чита́ю.**
Or simply:
**Чита́ю.**

He writes a letter. They write. You write.

**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вона́ слу́хає му́зику.**
We listen to the radio. She listens to music.

You can freely describe your workday. You know the necessary words to explain your full routine. Let us carefully review the patterns in a short reading block.

**Мій день.**
**Я працю́ю вдо́ма. Я працю́ю бага́то. Ми працю́ємо ра́зом.**
**Вона́ слу́хає му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ра́діо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
**Я чита́ю нови́ни. Він чита́є текст. Ми чита́ємо.**
**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву. Я вивча́ю слова́. Ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
**Я ду́маю. Ти розумі́єш. Вона́ зна́є пра́вду.**
**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус. Вони́ чека́ють.**
**По́тім ми відпочива́ємо вдо́ма. Я гра́ю. Вони́ гра́ють.**

My day.
I work at home. I work a lot. We work together.
She listens to music. You listen to the radio. They listen.
I read the news. He reads the text. We read.
You study the language. I study words. We know a lot.
I think. You understand. She knows the truth.
We wait for the bus. They wait.
Then we rest at home. I play. They play.

By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Вони́ вивча́ють.**
I understand. You understand. We study the language. They study.

**Ми ду́маємо бага́то. Вони́ ду́мають бага́то. Ти ду́маєш.**
We think a lot. They think a lot. You think.

You learned an important and highly useful rule today. This is a real foundational key to the language. It safely opens many new conversational possibilities.

The First Conjugation pattern is incredibly powerful. Once you thoroughly internalize the regular endings, you can instantly unlock hundreds of new verbs. You simply learn the dictionary infinitive form, and you immediately know exactly how to correctly use it in a conversation.

You learned how to clearly and confidently say what you do. You also learned the vital stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending effectively contains the subject information. 

> [!culture]
> **Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю. А люди́ну — по мо́ві.**
> A bird is known by its feathers. And a person — by their speech.
> There is a famously profound Ukrainian proverb about speaking properly. It broadly means that the way you speak vividly reveals your true character and education. Taking dedicated time to accurately learn correct endings shows deep respect for the language. 

We describe our daily actions every day. We actively talk about our regular habits. This is a big sign of clear progress.

You are no longer just quietly pointing at static objects in the room. You are actively describing dynamic actions and daily routines. This undeniably represents a truly massive step forward in your language learning journey. 

Here are a few useful practical questions for thoroughly reviewing the material.

1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun "we" in the First Conjugation? 
Answer: The correct ending is "-ємо". For example:
**Ми чита́ємо.**

2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun "I" in a sentence? 
Answer: No, the verb ending clearly indicates the exact subject, so you can smoothly drop the pronoun.

3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb "to read"? 
Answer: You drop the standard infinitive ending to safely get the stem.
**Чита-**

4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? 
**Я чита́ю.**
Or simply:
...

resultDisplay.originalContent:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: First Conjugation (-ати/-яти) in present tense, basic SVO sentences with inanimate objects.
Not covered:
  - Second Conjugation verbs → a1-16
  - Questions and negation → a1-18
-->

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Learning how to conjugate verbs unlocks your ability to describe actions and routines. Instead of simply naming static objects around you, you can now confidently say what you do with them every single day. This is the foundation of conversational fluency.

## Вступ (Introduction)

**Ми вивча́ємо слова́. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Тепе́р ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
We study words. We study the language. Now we know a lot.

**Я чита́ю кни́гу. Ти чита́єш нови́ни. Вона́ чита́є текст.**
I read a book. You read the news. She reads the text.

In this important lesson, you will learn how to describe your daily actions. A verb is an action word. In the Ukrainian language, verbs change their endings depending on who exactly is doing the action. This fundamental process of changing verb endings is called conjugation. It follows very logical and predictable patterns.

You will start with the most common and regular group of verbs. This large group is called the First Conjugation. Most of these verbs end in **-ати** in their dictionary form. This base form is called the infinitive. For example, the verb **чита́ти** is an infinitive. 

The verb **зна́ти** also belongs to this group. Another incredibly useful and high-frequency verb is **працюва́ти**. These verbs describe ongoing actions or regular daily habits. They cleanly tell us what you are doing right now, or what you usually do every single day. This specific concept is known as the imperfective aspect.

> [!culture]
> **Я чита́ю текст. Ми чита́ємо. Вони́ чита́ють.**
> I read the text. We read. They read.
> The verb **чита́ти** has deep roots in Ukrainian history. In the year 1574, a famous printer named Ivan Fedorov published a beautifully crafted book called the *Apostol* in Lviv. It was the very first printed book in Ukraine. Knowing how to read and write has always been highly valued in traditional Ukrainian culture.

We are starting a new language journey. It will be very interesting and useful.

## Презентація (Presentation)

**Я зна́ю пра́вило. Ми зна́ємо пра́вило. Ти зна́єш все. Вони́ зна́ють.**
I know the rule. We know the rule. You know everything. They know.

To use a verb in a spoken sentence, you must change its ending. This change tells the listener exactly who is performing the action. Let us look closely at the core pattern for First Conjugation verbs.

Think of the infinitive form as having two separate structural parts: the stem and the ending. For regular verbs like **чита́ти**, the infinitive ending is **-ти**. The stem is everything that comes right before that ending. For this specific word, the stem is **чита-**. 

To conjugate the verb correctly, you drop the **-ти** and add a personal ending. These specific endings always match the personal pronouns.

Here is the complete present tense pattern using the verb **чита́ти**:

| Pronoun | Stem | Ending | Full Verb | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **я** | чита- | **-ю** | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| **ти** | чита- | **-єш** | **ти чита́єш** | you read (singular) |
| **він/вона** | чита- | **-є** | **він/вона чита́є** | he/she reads |
| **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural) |
| **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |

Notice carefully that the regular endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can easily apply it to hundreds of everyday verbs. 

Now we apply this rule. We use the verb "to know". This is a very popular word.

**Я зна́ю все. Він зна́є пра́вду. Ми не зна́ємо. Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило.**
I know everything. He knows the truth. We do not know. They know the rule.

> [!tip]
> **Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Він розумі́є.**
> I understand. You understand. He understands.
> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. In Ukrainian, however, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** exclusively means "I". Because of this built-in grammatical information, you can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation.

The verb "to work" has a small change. The part "-юва-" shortens to just "-ю-". But the personal endings remain standard.

**Я працю́ю. Ти працю́єш. Вона́ працю́є.**
I work. You work. She works.

**Ми працю́ємо ра́зом. Вони́ працю́ють бага́то.**
We work together. They work a lot.

These verbs neatly describe continuous processes. The simple present tense smoothly covers both regular habits and current ongoing actions.

## Практика (Practice)

**Я слу́хаю му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ува́жно. Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
I listen to music. You listen carefully. We listen to the radio. They listen.

Now it is time for active practice. We are building new sentences. We are describing our daily actions using our target verbs. 

Many English speakers make a common structural mistake when they first start using Ukrainian verbs. They mistakenly say the infinitive when they actually mean the conjugated action. This is grammatically incorrect. You must always change the ending to match the person.

Do not use the infinitive form when speaking about actions. You must consistently use the properly conjugated form. 

When using the verb "to write", the correct form is slightly different. We speak like this.
**Я пишу́. Він пи́ше. Ми пи́шемо.**
I write. He writes. We write.

Looking at the verb "to listen", the correct form uses the standard rule.
**Я слу́хаю. Вона́ слу́хає. Вони́ слу́хають.**
I listen. She listens. They listen.

The verb "to play" also uses the standard rule perfectly.
**Я гра́ю. Ти гра́єш. Ми гра́ємо.**
I play. You play. We play.

Let us practice with a few more common verbs. Pay close attention to how the endings naturally change.

Another very common habit for English speakers is overusing pronouns. While repeating the pronoun is technically correct and understandable, it sounds highly repetitive. We read the text without extra words. It sounds much better.

**Чита́ю нови́ни. Працю́ю. Відпочива́ю.**
I read the news. I work. I rest.

**Слу́хаю ра́діо. Ду́маю. Чека́ю.**
I listen to the radio. I think. I wait.

> [!warning]
> **Я пита́ю про це. Він пита́є про це. Ми пита́ємо.**
> I ask about this. He asks about this. We ask.
> The highly useful verb "to ask" is a notable exception in how it connects to other words in a sentence. Pointing the action at a person requires a different and more complex case rule. For now, only use it safely with the phrase "about this".

Let us build simple, complete sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change at all when they become the direct target of an action.

**Я чита́ю журна́л. Ми чита́ємо текст.**
I read a magazine. We read the text.

**Він пи́ше лист. Вони́ пи́шуть.**
He writes a letter. They write.

**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вона́ слу́хає.**
We listen to the radio. She listens.

You can freely describe your workday. You know the necessary words to explain your full routine. Let us review the patterns in action.

**Я працю́ю бага́то. Ти працю́єш.**
I work a lot. You work.

**Я відпочива́ю вдо́ма. Вона́ відпочива́є. Ми відпочива́ємо.**
I rest at home. She rests. We rest.

**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус. Він чека́є.**
We wait for the bus. He waits.

**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву. Ми вивча́ємо слова́.**
You study the language. We study words.

**Я не розумі́ю. Вона́ розумі́є все.**
I do not understand. She understands everything.

By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Вони́ вивча́ють.**
I understand. You understand. We study the language. They study.

**Ми ду́маємо бага́то. Вони́ ду́мають бага́то. Ти ду́маєш.**
We think a lot. They think a lot. You think.

You learned an important and useful rule today. This is a real key to the language. It opens many conversational possibilities.

The First Conjugation pattern is incredibly powerful. Once you thoroughly internalize the regular endings, you can instantly unlock hundreds of verbs. You simply learn the infinitive form, and you immediately know exactly how to use it in a conversation.

You learned how to clearly confidently say what you do. You also learned the vital stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending contains the subject information. 

> [!culture]
> **Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю. А люди́ну — по мо́ві.**
> A bird is known by its feathers. And a person — by their speech.
> There is a famously profound Ukrainian proverb about speaking properly. It broadly means that the way you speak vividly reveals your true character and education. Taking dedicated time to learn correct endings shows deep respect. 

We describe our actions every day. We talk about our habits. This is a big progress.

You are no longer just quietly pointing at static objects. You are actively describing dynamic actions and routines. This represents a truly massive step forward in your learning journey. 

Here are a few useful questions for reviewing the material.

1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun "we" in the First Conjugation? 
Answer: The correct ending is "-ємо". For example:
**Ми чита́ємо.**

2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun "I" in a sentence? 
Answer: No, the verb ending clearly indicates the subject, so you can smoothly drop the pronoun.

3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb "to read"? 
Answer: You drop the infinitive ending to safely get the stem.
**Чита-**

4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? 
**Я чита́ю.**
Or simply:
**Чита́ю.**
resultDisplay.newContent:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: First Conjugation (-ати/-яти) in present tense, basic SVO sentences with inanimate objects.
Not covered:
  - Second Conjugation verbs → a1-16
  - Questions and negation → a1-18
-->

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Learning how to conjugate verbs unlocks your ability to describe actions and routines. Instead of simply naming static objects around you, you can now confidently say what you do with them every single day. This is the foundation of conversational fluency.

## Вступ (Introduction)

**Ми вивча́ємо слова́. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Тепе́р ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
We study words. We study the language. Now we know a lot.

**Я чита́ю кни́гу. Ти чита́єш нови́ни. Вона́ чита́є текст. Ми чита́ємо.**
I read a book. You read the news. She reads the text. We read.

In this important lesson, you will learn how to describe your daily actions. You will transition from simply naming objects to proudly stating what you actively do with them. A verb is an action word. In the Ukrainian language, verbs change their endings depending on who exactly is doing the action. This fundamental process of changing verb endings is called conjugation. It follows very logical and predictable patterns.

You will start with the most common and regular group of verbs. This large group is called the First Conjugation. Most of these verbs end in **-ати** in their dictionary form. This base form is called the infinitive. For example, the verb **чита́ти** is an infinitive. 

The verb **зна́ти** also belongs to this group. Another incredibly useful and high-frequency verb is **працюва́ти**. These verbs describe ongoing actions or regular daily habits. They cleanly tell us what you are doing right now, or what you usually do every single day. This specific concept is known as the imperfective aspect.

> [!culture]
> **Я чита́ю текст. Ми чита́ємо. Вони́ чита́ють.**
> I read the text. We read. They read.
> The verb **чита́ти** has deep roots in Ukrainian history. In the year 1574, a famous printer named Ivan Fedorov published a beautifully crafted book called the *Apostol* in Lviv. It was the very first printed book in Ukraine. Knowing how to read and write has always been highly valued in traditional Ukrainian culture.

We are starting a new language journey. It will be very interesting and useful.

## Презентація (Presentation)

**Я зна́ю пра́вило. Ми зна́ємо пра́вило. Ти зна́єш все. Вони́ зна́ють.**
I know the rule. We know the rule. You know everything. They know.

To use a verb in a spoken sentence, you must change its ending. This change tells the listener exactly who is performing the action. Let us look closely at the core pattern for First Conjugation verbs.

Think of the infinitive form as having two separate structural parts: the stem and the ending. For regular verbs like **чита́ти**, the infinitive ending is **-ти**. The stem is everything that comes right before that ending. For this specific word, the stem is **чита-**. 

To conjugate the verb correctly, you drop the **-ти** and add a personal ending. These specific endings always match the personal pronouns.

Here is the complete present tense pattern using the verb **чита́ти**:

| Pronoun | Stem | Ending | Full Verb | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **я** | чита- | **-ю** | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| **ти** | чита- | **-єш** | **ти чита́єш** | you read (singular) |
| **він/вона** | чита- | **-є** | **він/вона чита́є** | he/she reads |
| **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural) |
| **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |

Notice carefully that the regular endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can easily apply it to hundreds of everyday verbs. 

Now we apply this rule. We use the verb "to know". This is a very popular word.

**Я зна́ю все. Він зна́є пра́вду. Ми не зна́ємо. Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило.**
I know everything. He knows the truth. We do not know. They know the rule.

> [!tip]
> **Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Він розумі́є. Ми розумі́ємо.**
> I understand. You understand. He understands. We understand.
> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. In Ukrainian, however, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** exclusively means "I". Because of this built-in grammatical information, you can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation.

The verb "to work" has a small spelling change. The part "-юва-" shortens to just "-ю-". But the personal endings remain standard.

**Я працю́ю. Ти працю́єш. Вона́ працю́є.**
I work. You work. She works.

**Ми працю́ємо ра́зом. Вони́ працю́ють бага́то.**
We work together. They work a lot.

**Ми вивча́ємо слова́. Це нове́ пра́вило.**
We study words. This is a new rule.

**Він працю́є вдо́ма. Вони́ працю́ють.**
He works at home. They work.

These verbs neatly describe continuous processes. The simple present tense smoothly covers both regular habits and current ongoing actions. You do not need a separate tense for actions happening right now.

## Практика (Practice)

**Я слу́хаю му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ува́жно. Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
I listen to music. You listen carefully. We listen to the radio. They listen.

Now it is time for active practice. We are building new sentences. We are describing our daily actions using our target verbs. 

Many English speakers make a common structural mistake when they first start using Ukrainian verbs. They mistakenly say the infinitive when they actually mean the conjugated action. This is grammatically incorrect. You must always change the ending to match the person.

Do not use the infinitive form when speaking about actions. You must consistently use the properly conjugated form. 

When using the verb "to write", the correct form is slightly different. We speak like this.
**Я пишу́. Він пи́ше. Ми пи́шемо. Вони́ пи́шуть.**
I write. He writes. We write. They write.

Looking at the verb "to listen", the correct form uses the standard rule.
**Я слу́хаю. Вона́ слу́хає. Ми слу́хаємо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
I listen. She listens. We listen. They listen.

The verb "to play" also uses the standard rule perfectly.
**Я гра́ю. Ти гра́єш. Ми гра́ємо. Вони́ гра́ють.**
I play. You play. We play. They play.

Another very common habit for English speakers is overusing pronouns. While repeating the pronoun is technically correct and understandable, it sounds highly repetitive. We read the text without extra words. It sounds much better.

**Чита́ю нови́ни. Працю́ю. Відпочива́ю.**
I read the news. I work. I rest.

**Слу́хаю ра́діо. Ду́маю. Чека́ю.**
I listen to the radio. I think. I wait.

**Пишу́ лист. Чека́ю. Гра́ю.**
I write a letter. I wait. I play.

> [!warning]
> **Я пита́ю про це. Він пита́є про це. Ми пита́ємо.**
> I ask about this. He asks about this. We ask.
> The highly useful verb "to ask" is a notable exception in how it connects to other words in a sentence. Pointing the action at a person requires a different and more complex case rule. For now, only use it safely with the exact phrase "about this".

Let us build simple, complete sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change at all when they become the direct target of an action.

**Я чита́ю журна́л. Ми чита́ємо текст. Вона́ чита́є.**
I read a magazine. We read the text. She reads.

**Він пи́ше лист. Вони́ пи́шуть. Ти пи́шеш.**
He writes a letter. They write. You write.

**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вона́ слу́хає му́зику.**
We listen to the radio. She listens to music.

You can freely describe your workday. You know the necessary words to explain your full routine. Let us carefully review the patterns in a short reading block.

**Мій день.**
**Я працю́ю вдо́ма. Я працю́ю бага́то. Ми працю́ємо ра́зом.**
**Вона́ слу́хає му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ра́діо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
**Я чита́ю нови́ни. Він чита́є текст. Ми чита́ємо.**
**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву. Я вивча́ю слова́. Ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
**Я ду́маю. Ти розумі́єш. Вона́ зна́є пра́вду.**
**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус. Вони́ чека́ють.**
**По́тім ми відпочива́ємо вдо́ма. Я гра́ю. Вони́ гра́ють.**

My day.
I work at home. I work a lot. We work together.
She listens to music. You listen to the radio. They listen.
I read the news. He reads the text. We read.
You study the language. I study words. We know a lot.
I think. You understand. She knows the truth.
We wait for the bus. They wait.
Then we rest at home. I play. They play.

By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Вони́ вивча́ють.**
I understand. You understand. We study the language. They study.

**Ми ду́маємо бага́то. Вони́ ду́мають бага́то. Ти ду́маєш.**
We think a lot. They think a lot. You think.

You learned an important and highly useful rule today. This is a real foundational key to the language. It safely opens many new conversational possibilities.

The First Conjugation pattern is incredibly powerful. Once you thoroughly internalize the regular endings, you can instantly unlock hundreds of new verbs. You simply learn the dictionary infinitive form, and you immediately know exactly how to correctly use it in a conversation.

You learned how to clearly and confidently say what you do. You also learned the vital stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending effectively contains the subject information. 

> [!culture]
> **Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю. А люди́ну — по мо́ві.**
> A bird is known by its feathers. And a person — by their speech.
> There is a famously profound Ukrainian proverb about speaking properly. It broadly means that the way you speak vividly reveals your true character and education. Taking dedicated time to accurately learn correct endings shows deep respect for the language. 

We describe our daily actions every day. We actively talk about our regular habits. This is a big sign of clear progress.

You are no longer just quietly pointing at static objects in the room. You are actively describing dynamic actions and daily routines. This undeniably represents a truly massive step forward in your language learning journey. 

Here are a few useful practical questions for thoroughly reviewing the material.

1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun "we" in the First Conjugation? 
Answer: The correct ending is "-ємо". For example:
**Ми чита́ємо.**

2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun "I" in a sentence? 
Answer: No, the verb ending clearly indicates the exact subject, so you can smoothly drop the pronoun.

3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb "to read"? 
Answer: You drop the standard infinitive ending to safely get the stem.
**Чита-**

4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? 
**Я чита́ю.**
Or simply:
**Чита́ю.**

This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.

**Я чита́ю кни́гу. Ти чита́єш нови́ни. Вона́ чита́є текст. Ми чита́ємо.**

> I read the text. We read. They read.
> The verb **чита́ти** has deep roots. In 1574, Ivan Fedorov published a beautifully crafted book called the *Apostol* in Lviv. It was the very first printed book in Ukraine. 

We are starting an interesting language journey. 

## Презентація (Presentation)

**Я зна́ю пра́вило. Ми зна́ємо пра́вило. Ти зна́єш все. Вони́ зна́ють.**
I know the rule. We know the rule. You know everything. They know.

To use a verb in a spoken sentence, you must change its ending. This tells the listener exactly who is performing the action. 

Think of the infinitive form as having two parts: the stem and the ending. For regular verbs like **чита́ти**, the infinitive ending is **-ти**. The stem is everything before that ending: **чита-**.

Here is the complete present tense pattern using the verb **чита́ти**:

| Pronoun | Stem | Ending | Full Verb | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **я** | чита- | **-ю** | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| **ти** | чита- | **-єш** | **ти чита́єш** | you read (singular) |
| **він/вона** | чита- | **-є** | **він/вона чита́є** | he/she reads |
| **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural) |
| **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |

Notice carefully that the regular endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can apply it to hundreds of verbs. 

Now we apply this rule to the verb "to know".

**Я зна́ю все. Він зна́є пра́вду. Ми не зна́ємо. Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило.**
I know everything. He knows the truth. We do not know. They know the rule.

**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Він розумі́є. Ми розумі́ємо.**
I understand. You understand. He understands. We understand.

> [!tip]
> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. In Ukrainian, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** exclusively means "I". You can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation.

The verb "to work" has a small spelling change. The part "-юва-" shortens to just "-ю-". The personal endings remain standard.

**Я працю́ю. Ти працю́єш. Вона́ працю́є.**
I work. You work. She works.

**Ми працю́ємо ра́зом. Вони́ працю́ють бага́то.**
We work together. They work a lot.

**Ми вивча́ємо слова́. Це нове́ пра́вило.**
We study words. This is a new rule.

**Він працю́є вдо́ма. Вони́ працю́ють.**
He works at home. They work.

These verbs neatly describe continuous processes. The simple present tense covers both regular habits and current ongoing actions. You do not need a separate tense for actions happening right now.

## Практика (Practice)

**Я слу́хаю му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ува́жно. Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
I listen to music. You listen carefully. We listen to the radio. They listen.

Now it is time for active practice. We are building new sentences. We are describing our daily actions. 

Many English speakers make a common mistake. They mistakenly say the infinitive when they actually mean the conjugated action. You must always change the ending to match the person.

Do not use the infinitive form when speaking about actions. You must consistently use the properly conjugated form. 

When using the verb "to write", the correct form is slightly different. We speak like this.
**Я пишу́. Він пи́ше. Ми пи́шемо. Вони́ пи́шуть.**
I write. He writes. We write. They write.

Looking at the verb "to listen", the correct form uses the standard rule.
**Я слу́хаю. Вона́ слу́хає. Ми слу́хаємо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
I listen. She listens. We listen. They listen.

The verb "to play" also uses the standard rule perfectly.
**Я гра́ю. Ти гра́єш. Ми гра́ємо. Вони́ гра́ють.**
I play. You play. We play. They play.

Another common habit for English speakers is overusing pronouns. While repeating the pronoun is technically correct, it sounds repetitive. We read the text without extra words. It sounds much better.

**Чита́ю нови́ни. Працю́ю. Відпочива́ю.**
I read the news. I work. I rest.

**Слу́хаю ра́діо. Ду́маю. Чека́ю.**
I listen to the radio. I think. I wait.

**Пишу́ лист. Чека́ю. Гра́ю.**
I write a letter. I wait. I play.

> [!warning]
> **Я пита́ю про це. Він пита́є про це. Ми пита́ємо.**
> I ask about this. He asks about this. We ask.
> The highly useful verb "to ask" is a notable exception in how it connects to other words in a sentence. For now, only use it safely with the exact phrase "about this".

Let us build simple sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change.

**Я чита́ю журна́л. Ми чита́ємо текст. Вона́ чита́є.**
I read a magazine. We read the text. She reads.

**Він пи́ше лист. Вони́ пи́шуть. Ти пи́шеш.**
He writes a letter. They write. You write.

**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вона́ слу́хає му́зику.**
We listen to the radio. She listens to music.

You can freely describe your workday. Let us carefully review the patterns in a short reading block.

**Мій день. Я працю́ю вдо́ма.**
My day. I work at home.

**Я працю́ю бага́то. Ми працю́ємо ра́зом.**
I work a lot. We work together.

**Вона́ слу́хає му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ра́діо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
She listens to music. You listen to the radio. They listen.

**Я чита́ю нови́ни. Він чита́є текст. Ми чита́ємо.**
I read the news. He reads the text. We read.

**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву. Я вивча́ю слова́. Ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
You study the language. I study words. We know a lot.

**Я ду́маю. Ти розумі́єш. Вона́ зна́є пра́вду.**
I think. You understand. She knows the truth.

**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус. Вони́ чека́ють.**
We wait for the bus. They wait.

**По́тім ми відпочива́ємо вдо́ма. Я гра́ю. Вони́ гра́ють.**
Then we rest at home. I play. They play.

By confidently combining these verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate practical information.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Вони́ вивча́ють.**
I understand. You understand. We study the language. They study.

**Ми ду́маємо бага́то. Вони́ ду́мають бага́то. Ти ду́маєш.**
We think a lot. They think a lot. You think.

You learned an important and highly useful rule today. This is a real foundational key to the language. 

The First Conjugation pattern is incredibly powerful. Once you thoroughly internalize the regular endings, you can instantly unlock hundreds of new verbs. You simply learn the dictionary infinitive form, and you immediately know exactly how to correctly use it.

You learned how to confidently say what you do. You also learned the vital stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns because the verb ending effectively contains the subject information. 

> [!culture]
> **Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю. А люди́ну — по мо́ві.**
> A bird is known by its feathers. And a person — by their speech.
> There is a profoundly beautiful Ukrainian proverb about speaking properly. It broadly means that the way you speak vividly reveals your true character and education. Taking dedicated time to accurately learn correct endings shows deep respect for the language. 

We describe our daily actions every day. We actively talk about our regular habits. This is a big sign of clear progress.

You are no longer just quietly pointing at static objects in the room. You are actively describing dynamic actions and daily routines. This undeniably represents a truly massive step forward in your language learning journey. 

Here are a few useful practical questions for thoroughly reviewing the material.

1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun "we" in the First Conjugation? 
Answer: The correct ending is "-ємо". For example:
**Ми чита́ємо.**

2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun "I" in a sentence? 
Answer: No, the verb ending clearly indicates the exact subject, so you can smoothly drop the pronoun.

3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb "to read"? 
Answer: You drop the standard infinitive ending to safely get the stem.
**Чита-**

4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? 
**Я чита́ю.**
Or simply:
**Чита́ю.**

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>

## Вступ (Introduction)

**Ми вивча́ємо слова́. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Тепе́р ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
We study words. We study the language. Now we know a lot.

**Я чита́ю кни́гу. Ти чита́єш нови́ни. Вона́ чита́є текст. Ми чита́ємо.**
I read a book. You read the news. She reads the text. We read.

In this lesson, you will learn to describe your daily actions. A verb is an action word. In Ukrainian, verbs change their endings depending on who exactly is doing the action. This fundamental process is called conjugation. It follows predictable patterns.

You will start with the First Conjugation. Most of these verbs end in **-ати** in their dictionary form. This base form is called the infinitive. For example, the verb **чита́ти** is an infinitive. 

The verb **зна́ти** belongs to this group. Another highly useful verb is **працюва́ти**. These verbs cleanly describe ongoing actions or regular daily habits. This specific concept is known as the imperfective aspect.

> [!culture]
> **Я чита́ю текст. Ми чита́ємо. Вони́ чита́ють.**
> I read the text. We read. They read.
> The verb **чита́ти** has deep roots. In 1574, Ivan Fedorov published a beautifully crafted book called the *Apostol* in Lviv. It was the very first printed book in Ukraine. 

We are starting an interesting language journey. 

## Презентація (Presentation)

**Я зна́ю пра́вило. Ми зна́ємо пра́вило. Ти зна́єш все. Вони́ зна́ють.**
I know the rule. We know the rule. You know everything. They know.

To use a verb in a spoken sentence, you must change its ending. This tells the listener exactly who is performing the action. 

Think of the infinitive form as having two parts: the stem and the ending. For regular verbs like **чита́ти**, the infinitive ending is **-ти**. The stem is everything before that ending: **чита-**. 

To conjugate the verb, you drop the **-ти** and add a personal ending. These specific endings always match the personal pronouns.

Here is the complete present tense pattern using the verb **чита́ти**:

| Pronoun | Stem | Ending | Full Verb | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
...
| **він/вона** | чита- | **-є** | **він/вона чита́є** | he/she reads |
| **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural) |
| **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |

Notice carefully that the regular endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can apply it to hundreds of verbs. 

Now we apply this rule to the verb "to know".

**Я зна́ю все. Він зна́є пра́вду. Ми не зна́ємо. Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило.**
I know everything. He knows the truth. We do not know. They know the rule.

**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Він розумі́є. Ми розумі́ємо.**
I understand. You understand. He understands. We understand.

> [!tip]
> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. In Ukrainian, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** exclusively means "I". You can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation.

The verb "to work" has a small spelling change. The part "-юва-" shortens to just "-ю-". The personal endings remain standard.

**Я працю́ю. Ти працю́єш. Вона́ працю́є.**
I work. You work. She works.

**Ми працю́ємо ра́зом. Вони́ працю́ють бага́то.**
...
We study words. This is a new rule.

**Він працю́є вдо́ма. Вони́ працю́ють.**
He works at home. They work.

These verbs neatly describe continuous processes. The simple present tense covers both regular habits and current ongoing actions. You do not need a separate tense for actions happening right now.

<!-- SCOPE
Covers: First Conjugation (-ати/-яти) in present tense, basic SVO sentences with inanimate objects.
Not covered:
  - Second Conjugation verbs → a1-16
  - Questions and negation → a1-18

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Learning how to conjugate verbs unlocks your ability to describe actions and routines. Instead of simply naming static objects around you, you can now confidently say what you do with them every single day. This is the foundation of conversational fluency.

## Вступ (Introduction)

**Ми вивча́ємо слова́. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Тепе́р ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
We study words. We study the language. Now we know a lot.

**Я чита́ю кни́гу. Ти чита́єш нови́ни. Вона́ чита́є текст. Ми чита́ємо.**
I read a book. You read the news. She reads the text. We read.

In this important lesson, you will learn how to describe your daily actions. You will transition from simply naming objects to proudly stating what you actively do with them. A verb is an action word. In the Ukrainian language, verbs change their endings depending on who exactly is doing the action. This fundamental process of changing verb endings is called conjugation. It follows very logical and predictable patterns.

You will start with the most common and regular group of verbs. This large group is called the First Conjugation. Most of these verbs end in **-ати** in their dictionary form. This base form is called the infinitive. For example, the verb **чита́ти** is an infinitive. 

The verb **зна́ти** also belongs to this group. Another incredibly useful and high-frequency verb is **працюва́ти**. These verbs describe ongoing actions or regular daily habits. They cleanly tell us what you are doing right now, or what you usually do every single day. This specific concept is known as the imperfective aspect.

> [!culture]
> **Я чита́ю текст. Ми чита́ємо. Вони́ чита́ють.**
> I read the text. We read. They read.
> The verb **чита́ти** has deep roots in Ukrainian history. In the year 1574, a famous printer named Ivan Fedorov published a beautifully crafted book called the *Apostol* in Lviv. It was the very first printed book in Ukraine. Knowing how to read and write has always been highly valued in traditional Ukrainian culture.

We are starting a new language journey. It will be very interesting and useful.

## Презентація (Presentation)

**Я зна́ю пра́вило. Ми зна́ємо пра́вило. Ти зна́єш все. Вони́ зна́ють.**
I know the rule. We know the rule. You know everything. They know.

To use a verb in a spoken sentence, you must change its ending. This change tells the listener exactly who is performing the action. Let us look closely at the core pattern for First Conjugation verbs.

Think of the infinitive form as having two separate structural parts: the stem and the ending. For regular verbs like **чита́ти**, the infinitive ending is **-ти**. The stem is everything that comes right before that ending. For this specific word, the stem is **чита-**. 

To conjugate the verb correctly, you drop the **-ти** and add a personal ending. These specific endings always match the personal pronouns.

Here is the complete present tense pattern using the verb **чита́ти**:

| Pronoun | Stem | Ending | Full Verb | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **я** | чита- | **-ю** | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| **ти** | чита- | **-єш** | **ти чита́єш** | you read (singular) |
| **він/вона** | чита- | **-є** | **він/вона чита́є** | he/she reads |
| **ми** | чита- | **-ємо** | **ми чита́ємо** | we read |
| **ви** | чита- | **-єте** | **ви чита́єте** | you read (plural) |
| **вони** | чита- | **-ють** | **вони чита́ють** | they read |

Notice carefully that the regular endings are **-ю**, **-єш**, **-є**, **-ємо**, **-єте**, and **-ють**. This grammatical pattern is extremely reliable. You can easily apply it to hundreds of everyday verbs. 

Now we apply this rule. We use the verb "to know". This is a very popular word.

**Я зна́ю все. Він зна́є пра́вду. Ми не зна́ємо. Вони́ зна́ють пра́вило.**
I know everything. He knows the truth. We do not know. They know the rule.

> [!tip]
> **Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Він розумі́є. Ми розумі́ємо.**
> I understand. You understand. He understands. We understand.
> In English, you must almost always use a pronoun. In Ukrainian, however, the verb ending itself already tells us exactly who is doing the action. The ending **-ю** exclusively means "I". Because of this built-in grammatical information, you can often drop the pronoun completely. Saying **Чита́ю** is a perfectly natural and common way to say "I am reading" in everyday conversation.

The verb "to work" has a small spelling change. The part "-юва-" shortens to just "-ю-". But the personal endings remain standard.

**Я працю́ю. Ти працю́єш. Вона́ працю́є.**
I work. You work. She works.

**Ми працю́ємо ра́зом. Вони́ працю́ють бага́то.**
We work together. They work a lot.

**Ми вивча́ємо слова́. Це нове́ пра́вило.**
We study words. This is a new rule.

**Він працю́є вдо́ма. Вони́ працю́ють.**
He works at home. They work.

These verbs neatly describe continuous processes. The simple present tense smoothly covers both regular habits and current ongoing actions. You do not need a separate tense for actions happening right now.

## Практика (Practice)

**Я слу́хаю му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ува́жно. Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
I listen to music. You listen carefully. We listen to the radio. They listen.

Now it is time for active practice. We are building new sentences. We are describing our daily actions using our target verbs. 

Many English speakers make a common structural mistake when they first start using Ukrainian verbs. They mistakenly say the infinitive when they actually mean the conjugated action. This is grammatically incorrect. You must always change the ending to match the person.

Do not use the infinitive form when speaking about actions. You must consistently use the properly conjugated form. 

When using the verb "to write", the correct form is slightly different. We speak like this.
**Я пишу́. Він пи́ше. Ми пи́шемо. Вони́ пи́шуть.**
I write. He writes. We write. They write.

Looking at the verb "to listen", the correct form uses the standard rule.
**Я слу́хаю. Вона́ слу́хає. Ми слу́хаємо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
I listen. She listens. We listen. They listen.

The verb "to play" also uses the standard rule perfectly.
**Я гра́ю. Ти гра́єш. Ми гра́ємо. Вони́ гра́ють.**
I play. You play. We play. They play.

Another very common habit for English speakers is overusing pronouns. While repeating the pronoun is technically correct and understandable, it sounds highly repetitive. We read the text without extra words. It sounds much better.

**Чита́ю нови́ни. Працю́ю. Відпочива́ю.**
I read the news. I work. I rest.

**Слу́хаю ра́діо. Ду́маю. Чека́ю.**
I listen to the radio. I think. I wait.

**Пишу́ лист. Чека́ю. Гра́ю.**
I write a letter. I wait. I play.

> [!warning]
> **Я пита́ю про це. Він пита́є про це. Ми пита́ємо.**
> I ask about this. He asks about this. We ask.
> The highly useful verb "to ask" is a notable exception in how it connects to other words in a sentence. Pointing the action at a person requires a different and more complex case rule. For now, only use it safely with the exact phrase "about this".

Let us build simple, complete sentences with direct objects. You can safely use inanimate masculine and neuter objects very easily right now. Their dictionary form does not change at all when they become the direct target of an action.

**Я чита́ю журна́л. Ми чита́ємо текст. Вона́ чита́є.**
I read a magazine. We read the text. She reads.

**Він пи́ше лист. Вони́ пи́шуть. Ти пи́шеш.**
He writes a letter. They write. You write.

**Ми слу́хаємо ра́діо. Вона́ слу́хає му́зику.**
We listen to the radio. She listens to music.

You can freely describe your workday. You know the necessary words to explain your full routine. Let us carefully review the patterns in a short reading block.

**Мій день.**
**Я працю́ю вдо́ма. Я працю́ю бага́то. Ми працю́ємо ра́зом.**
**Вона́ слу́хає му́зику. Ти слу́хаєш ра́діо. Вони́ слу́хають.**
**Я чита́ю нови́ни. Він чита́є текст. Ми чита́ємо.**
**Ти вивча́єш мо́ву. Я вивча́ю слова́. Ми зна́ємо бага́то.**
**Я ду́маю. Ти розумі́єш. Вона́ зна́є пра́вду.**
**Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус. Вони́ чека́ють.**
**По́тім ми відпочива́ємо вдо́ма. Я гра́ю. Вони́ гра́ють.**

My day.
I work at home. I work a lot. We work together.
She listens to music. You listen to the radio. They listen.
I read the news. He reads the text. We read.
You study the language. I study words. We know a lot.
I think. You understand. She knows the truth.
We wait for the bus. They wait.
Then we rest at home. I play. They play.

By confidently combining these First Conjugation verbs with simple everyday objects, you can already communicate a massive amount of practical, real-world information.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

**Я розумі́ю. Ти розумі́єш. Ми вивча́ємо мо́ву. Вони́ вивча́ють.**
I understand. You understand. We study the language. They study.

**Ми ду́маємо бага́то. Вони́ ду́мають бага́то. Ти ду́маєш.**
We think a lot. They think a lot. You think.

You learned an important and highly useful rule today. This is a real foundational key to the language. It safely opens many new conversational possibilities.

The First Conjugation pattern is incredibly powerful. Once you thoroughly internalize the regular endings, you can instantly unlock hundreds of new verbs. You simply learn the dictionary infinitive form, and you immediately know exactly how to correctly use it in a conversation.

You learned how to clearly and confidently say what you do. You also learned the vital stylistic rule that you can frequently drop the pronouns altogether because the verb ending effectively contains the subject information. 

> [!culture]
> **Пти́цю пізна́ти по пі́р'ю. А люди́ну — по мо́ві.**
> A bird is known by its feathers. And a person — by their speech.
> There is a famously profound Ukrainian proverb about speaking properly. It broadly means that the way you speak vividly reveals your true character and education. Taking dedicated time to accurately learn correct endings shows deep respect for the language. 

We describe our daily actions every day. We actively talk about our regular habits. This is a big sign of clear progress.

You are no longer just quietly pointing at static objects in the room. You are actively describing dynamic actions and daily routines. This undeniably represents a truly massive step forward in your language learning journey. 

Here are a few useful practical questions for thoroughly reviewing the material.

1. What is the correct grammatical ending for the pronoun "we" in the First Conjugation? 
Answer: The correct ending is "-ємо". For example:
**Ми чита́ємо.**

2. Do you always strictly need to use the pronoun "I" in a sentence? 
Answer: No, the verb ending clearly indicates the exact subject, so you can smoothly drop the pronoun.

3. How do you structurally form the base stem of the verb "to read"? 
Answer: You drop the standard infinitive ending to safely get the stem.
**Чита-**

4. How do you correctly and naturally say "I am reading" in conversational Ukrainian? 
**Я чита́ю.**
Or simply:
**Чита́ю.**
